syllabus_desc: Define: static pressure
concept: Static pressure is the plain ambient pressure of the atmosphere around you — the weight of all the air sitting on top of your aircraft at your current altitude. It pushes equally in every direction and doesn't care how fast you're going. At sea level ISA it's 1013.25 hPa. Climb a bit and it drops. Sit still on the Ardmore apron with the engine off and static pressure is the only thing your pitot-static system sees.
exam_tip: Static pressure = ambient air pressure at your location. Acts equally in all directions. Independent of aircraft motion.
key_facts: Ambient atmospheric pressure around the aircraft
Acts equally in all directions
Independent of aircraft speed or motion
1013.25 hPa at ISA sea level
Sensed through the static port
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Define: dynamic pressure
concept: Dynamic pressure is the extra pressure you feel because you're moving through the air (or the air is moving past you). Stick your hand out the window of a moving car and that push you feel isn't static pressure — it's dynamic. The formula is q = ½ρV², which tells you two things straight up: dynamic pressure depends on air density (ρ) and it depends on the square of the true airspeed (V). Double your speed and you quadruple the dynamic pressure. It's the bit of pressure the ASI actually cares about.
exam_tip: Dynamic pressure q = ½ρV². Depends on density and on the SQUARE of TAS. Only exists when there's relative motion between aircraft and air.
key_facts: Pressure arising from aircraft motion through the air
Formula q = ½ρV²
Varies with air density and square of TAS
Only exists when there's relative motion
Zero when the aircraft is stationary
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Define: total (or pitot) pressure
concept: Total pressure (also called pitot pressure or impact pressure) is what you get when you bring moving air to a sudden stop — it's static pressure plus dynamic pressure. The pitot tube does exactly that: the air rams into the open end, stops inside the tube, and the sensor measures what's there. So total pressure = static + dynamic. The ASI subtracts the static value and what's left is the dynamic pressure it converts into an airspeed reading.
exam_tip: Total (pitot) pressure = static + dynamic. It's what the pitot tube measures by stopping the airflow. The ASI then subtracts static to recover dynamic pressure.
key_facts: Total pressure = static + dynamic
Also called pitot pressure or impact pressure
Measured by the pitot tube where airflow is brought to rest
ASI subtracts static to get dynamic component
Forms the basis of airspeed indication
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between the following: static pressure
concept: When you're asked to distinguish the three pressures, the first point is that static pressure exists whether you're flying or parked. It's measured through the static port — a flush hole in the side of the fuselage where the airflow is smooth and parallel to the surface, so only the ambient pressure is sensed. The static port feeds the altimeter and VSI directly, and supplies the static side of the ASI diaphragm capsule.
exam_tip: Static pressure is ambient, always present, sensed through the flush static port. Feeds altimeter, VSI and static side of the ASI.
key_facts: Sensed at the flush static port
Ambient, independent of motion
Feeds altimeter, VSI and ASI static side
Static port is flush with fuselage to avoid dynamic contamination
Present even when the aircraft is stationary
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between the following: dynamic pressure
concept: Dynamic pressure is the difference between total and static — it's the part of the pitot pressure that's there only because the aircraft is moving. It's never sensed on its own by any single port; the ASI creates it mechanically by putting total pressure on one side of its capsule and static pressure on the other. Whatever's left is the dynamic pressure, which the ASI then scales into knots.
exam_tip: Dynamic pressure is never measured directly — it's total minus static, produced inside the ASI capsule.
key_facts: Dynamic = Total − Static
Never sensed directly by any one port
Produced inside the ASI by subtracting static from total
Drives the ASI capsule expansion
Proportional to ½ρV²
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between the following: total (or pitot) pressure
concept: Total (pitot) pressure is sensed at the pitot tube — an open-ended probe pointed into the airflow. The air piles up inside the tube and stops, so the pressure inside is static plus dynamic. It's only fed to one instrument: the ASI. The altimeter and VSI couldn't care less about pitot pressure — they only need static.
exam_tip: Pitot pressure is total pressure sensed at the pitot tube. Feeds the ASI only. Altimeter and VSI don't use it.
key_facts: Sensed at the pitot tube
Total = static + dynamic
Feeds the ASI only
Pitot tube points into the relative airflow
Not used by the altimeter or VSI
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With the aid of diagrams: identify the elements of a basic pitot-static system
concept: A basic pitot-static system has four main bits. A pitot tube pointing into the airflow gathers total pressure. One or more static ports (usually paired left and right to cancel sideslip effects) sample ambient static pressure. Plumbing lines — tubes or hoses — carry both pressures back into the cockpit. And the instruments: the ASI takes both total and static, the altimeter takes static only, the VSI takes static only. Most aircraft also have a pitot heater, a drain, and an alternate static source as part of the system. Draw it with arrows showing airflow into the pitot, flush static ports on the fuselage, and lines going to each instrument and you've nailed the diagram.
exam_tip: Basic elements: pitot tube, static port(s), plumbing lines, three instruments (ASI, altimeter, VSI), plus pitot heater, drains, and alternate static source.
key_facts: Pitot tube gathers total pressure
Static ports (often paired) gather static pressure
Plumbing lines feed the instruments
ASI uses both total and static; altimeter and VSI use static only
Pitot heater, drains and alternate static source are standard extras
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With the aid of diagrams: label the basic elements of a typical pitot-static probe
concept: A typical pitot-static probe has an open forward-facing hole (the pitot hole) to capture total pressure, a drain hole at the bottom to let water escape, and the heater element wrapped around the body to stop ice forming. Combined pitot-static probes also have small flush holes on the sides of the probe for static sensing. Labels for a diagram: pitot opening, drain hole, heating element, body/mast, static holes (if combined), mounting base.
exam_tip: Label a typical probe with: pitot opening, drain hole, heating element, static holes (if combined), mounting mast.
key_facts: Forward-facing pitot hole captures total pressure
Drain hole at the bottom clears water
Heating element prevents ice formation
Combined probes include flush static holes on the sides
Mast/mounting base attaches the probe to the aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between separate pitot probe/static vents, and a combined pitot-static probe
concept: Two arrangements for picking up static and pitot pressure. First, separate pitot probe and static vents — the pitot tube is on its own (usually under a wing or the nose) and static is sampled through flush vents on the fuselage sides, often paired left and right so that sideslip averages out. Second, a combined pitot-static probe — one probe does both jobs, with the forward-facing hole for pitot and flush side holes for static. The combined type is neater and keeps both sources in the same airflow, but it's more vulnerable to a single point of failure (one bent probe and you lose both pitot and static). Separate arrangements are common on trainers because they're simpler and cheaper.
exam_tip: Separate = pitot tube plus flush fuselage static vents (usually paired). Combined = one probe doing both. Combined is tidier but a single point of failure.
key_facts: Separate arrangement: pitot tube plus flush static vents on the fuselage
Paired static vents cancel sideslip error
Combined probe: single unit senses both pressures
Combined type is more compact but single-point failure
Separate arrangements are common on trainers
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the precautions and correct method of operation of the pitot heater
concept: The pitot heater is a simple electric element that stops ice forming inside the pitot tube (and on combined probes, the static holes too). Rules to live by: turn it on before entering visible moisture or cloud, and turn it off on the ground once you're parked — because the heater element can overheat quickly in still air and burn itself out. Most pilots run a pitot heat check during the runup by briefly switching it on and feeling the probe warm up, but never leave it on for long on the ground. Some aircraft have an ammeter that shows current draw when the heater is live. If you fly into known icing and forget to turn it on, you may find the ASI reading incorrectly or sticking within a couple of minutes.
exam_tip: Turn pitot heat ON before entering cloud or visible moisture. Turn it OFF on the ground promptly — it can overheat. Check operation briefly during runup.
key_facts: Electric heating element prevents pitot and static icing
Switch ON before entering visible moisture or cloud
Switch OFF promptly on the ground to prevent overheating
Check operation briefly during runup
Forgetting it in icing conditions leads to frozen or sticking ASI
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With respect to the airspeed indicator (ASI): explain the basic principle of operation
concept: The ASI is basically a differential pressure gauge with a dial. Inside the casing there's a sealed capsule connected to the pitot line (so it gets total pressure) and the casing itself is connected to the static line (so static pressure sits around the outside of the capsule). The capsule expands when total pressure exceeds static pressure — that difference is the dynamic pressure. A linkage translates capsule expansion into needle movement on the dial, scaled in knots. No flow, no dynamic pressure, no expansion, no indication. Speed up and the capsule pushes harder on the linkage, and the needle climbs.
exam_tip: ASI is a differential pressure gauge: pitot feeds the capsule, static fills the case. The capsule expands with dynamic pressure and drives the needle.
key_facts: Differential pressure gauge inside a sealed case
Capsule connected to pitot (total pressure)
Case connected to static (ambient pressure)
Capsule expansion = dynamic pressure
Linkage converts expansion to airspeed indication in knots
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With respect to the ASI: identify the markings on a typical light twin-engine aeroplane ASI
concept: A typical light twin ASI dial has colour-coded arcs and lines that tell you the whole speed envelope at a glance. The white arc runs from VS0 (stall speed in landing configuration) up to VFE (maximum flap extended speed). The green arc runs from VS1 (clean stall) up to VNO (maximum structural cruise speed). The yellow arc runs from VNO up to VNE (never exceed speed) — you only fly in this arc in smooth air. A red radial line sits at VNE, marking 'don't go past this ever'. A twin also has a blue radial line marking VYSE (best single-engine rate of climb, or blueline). And a red radial line for VMCA (minimum control speed, air) — below this with one engine out you lose directional control. The markings let you read the aircraft's speed envelope without opening the POH.
exam_tip: White arc: VS0 to VFE (flap operating range). Green arc: VS1 to VNO (normal). Yellow arc: VNO to VNE (caution, smooth air only). Red line: VNE. Blueline: VYSE (twin). Red radial: VMCA (twin).
key_facts: White arc: VS0 to VFE -- flap operating range
Green arc: VS1 to VNO -- normal operating range
Yellow arc: VNO to VNE -- caution, smooth air only
Red line at VNE: never exceed
Blueline at VYSE; red radial at VMCA on twins
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With respect to the ASI: state the relationship between indicated, calibrated, equivalent and true airspeeds (IAS, CAS, EAS and TAS)
concept: Four airspeeds, each a refinement of the last. IAS is what the ASI actually shows — raw dynamic pressure converted to a speed assuming ISA sea level conditions. CAS is IAS corrected for position and instrument errors (the POH has a table or graph). EAS is CAS corrected for compressibility — only matters above about 200 knots or so, irrelevant at PPL speeds. TAS is CAS (or EAS) corrected for density altitude — as you climb, air gets thinner, and your true speed through the air is higher than your indicated. A handy rule of thumb: TAS is roughly 2% higher than CAS per 1,000 ft of altitude. So flying an ASI reading of 100 knots at 8,000 ft on a warm day, your true airspeed is closer to 116 knots.
exam_tip: IAS → (+position/instrument error) → CAS → (+compressibility, mostly ignorable at PPL) → EAS → (+density altitude) → TAS. Rule of thumb: TAS ≈ CAS + 2% per 1,000 ft.
key_facts: IAS: raw ASI reading
CAS: IAS corrected for position and instrument error
EAS: CAS corrected for compressibility (matters above ~200 kt)
TAS: CAS (or EAS) corrected for density altitude
Rule of thumb: TAS ≈ CAS + 2% per 1,000 ft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With respect to the ASI: explain the effect of blockages and leaks, and the remedies available to the pilot
concept: A blocked pitot line leaves the capsule with whatever pressure was trapped inside. If you climb, static pressure drops while pitot pressure stays constant — so the difference grows and the ASI over-reads (acts like an altimeter). Descend and it under-reads. A leak in the pitot line lets total pressure bleed down to static levels, so the ASI reads low or zero. A blocked static line locks static pressure at whatever it was when the blockage happened — climb and the ASI under-reads, descend and it over-reads. A leak in the static line (inside a pressurised cabin) lets cabin pressure — which is usually higher than ambient — reach the instrument, making the ASI under-read and the altimeter over-read. The remedy for blocked static is the alternate static source (most aircraft have one), which vents the system to cabin air. Blocked pitot has no simple cockpit remedy in most trainers — you have to fly the known pitch and power attitude until you can land.
exam_tip: Blocked pitot: ASI behaves like altimeter (reads high in climb, low in descent). Blocked static: opposite (reads low in climb, high in descent). Use alternate static source for blocked static.
key_facts: Blocked pitot: ASI over-reads in climb, under-reads in descent (behaves like altimeter)
Leaking pitot: ASI under-reads or drops to zero
Blocked static: ASI under-reads in climb, over-reads in descent
Leaking static in pressurised cabin: ASI under-reads, altimeter over-reads
Alternate static source is the remedy for blocked static
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With respect to the ASI: state the serviceability checks
concept: Serviceability checks on the ASI start before the flight and continue on the takeoff roll. On the walkaround, check the pitot tube is clear and uncovered, the static ports are clear, and the drain holes aren't blocked. In the cockpit before taxi, confirm the ASI reads zero (a needle hung up off zero is a problem). During the runup, cycle the pitot heat briefly. On the takeoff roll, make sure the ASI is alive and increasing as expected — and call out 'airspeed alive' if that's your procedure. If it's not reading by about 30 knots on a typical trainer, reject the takeoff. In flight, cross-check against known pitch and power to catch a gradual leak or slow blockage.
exam_tip: Pre-flight: pitot clear, static ports clear, drain holes clear, ASI reads zero. Runup: pitot heat. Takeoff: airspeed alive call. In flight: cross-check with pitch and power.
key_facts: Pre-flight: check pitot and static clear, drain holes clear
On the ground: ASI reads zero
Runup: check pitot heat operation
Takeoff roll: confirm 'airspeed alive'
In flight: cross-check with pitch and power
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the ASI: instrument error
concept: Instrument error on the ASI comes from the mechanical quirks of that particular gauge — linkage friction, slight calibration drift, manufacturing tolerances. It's small and usually constant across the normal speed range. The POH has an IAS to CAS correction table that accounts for it alongside position error. You can't correct it in flight; you just accept it and use the POH numbers when accuracy matters (like short-field performance calculations).
exam_tip: Instrument error = mechanical quirks of the ASI. Small, constant, corrected via POH IAS/CAS table.
key_facts: Arises from mechanical tolerances and linkage friction
Small and relatively constant
Corrected via POH IAS/CAS table
Can't be fixed in flight
Combined with position error in most POH corrections
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the ASI: position error
concept: Position error (also called pressure error) comes from the pitot and static ports being in a slightly imperfect place on the airframe. At different angles of attack and different speeds, the local airflow around each port doesn't match the free-stream, so the pressures sensed are slightly off. It's usually worst at slow flight with high angle of attack — which is exactly when you don't want ASI errors. Position error is tabulated in the POH IAS/CAS correction chart and varies with flap setting, weight and configuration.
exam_tip: Position error = airflow around the ports isn't quite ideal. Worst at low speed/high AoA. Correction varies with configuration and is in the POH.
key_facts: Arises from imperfect port location in local airflow
Largest at high angle of attack / low speed
Varies with configuration (flap, weight)
Tabulated in POH IAS/CAS correction chart
Combined with instrument error in POH corrections
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the ASI: compressibility error
concept: Compressibility error happens because at high speeds the air actually compresses in front of the pitot tube, raising the total pressure sensed above what the simple ½ρV² formula predicts. The ASI doesn't know about this and over-reads. It's negligible below about 200 knots, which is to say you'll never worry about it in a Cessna 172, but it becomes significant in jets and fast turboprops. The correction takes CAS to EAS and the formulas live in performance manuals for high-speed aircraft.
exam_tip: Compressibility error: ASI over-reads at high speed because air compresses in the pitot. Negligible below ~200 kt. CAS → EAS correction.
key_facts: Air compression in the pitot raises sensed total pressure
ASI over-reads at high speeds
Negligible below ~200 kt
Significant in jets and fast turboprops
Correction takes CAS to EAS
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the ASI: density error
concept: Density error is the big one for PPL pilots. The ASI is calibrated to ISA sea level conditions (15°C, 1013.25 hPa, density 1.225 kg/m³). Climb to altitude or fly on a warm day and the air gets less dense — your ASI is still converting dynamic pressure to speed using the sea level number, so it under-reads compared to your true speed through the air. The correction takes CAS to TAS. Rule of thumb: add 2% per 1,000 ft of altitude. So at 10,000 ft on a standard day, your TAS is about 20% higher than CAS. This is why an IAS of 100 knots at Queenstown (elevation 1,171 ft) doesn't mean the same thing as 100 IAS at Ardmore.
exam_tip: Density error: ASI under-reads at altitude because air is less dense than sea level calibration. Correction is CAS → TAS. Rule of thumb: +2% per 1,000 ft.
key_facts: ASI calibrated to ISA sea level density
Less dense air means ASI under-reads compared to TAS
Correction is CAS → TAS
Rule of thumb: +2% per 1,000 ft
Biggest of the four errors at PPL/CPL altitudes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With respect to the altimeter: explain the basic principle of operation
concept: The altimeter is a sealed aneroid capsule (partial vacuum inside) sitting in the casing, which is connected to the static line. As you climb, static pressure drops, the case pressure drops, and the sealed capsule expands. A linkage translates that expansion into needle movement, scaled in feet of altitude. Descend and the capsule is squashed by the higher static pressure and the needle winds back down. The subscale (the Kollsman window) lets you bias the whole thing for different QNH values — setting a new QNH moves the scale so that the altimeter reads altitude above the datum you've chosen.
exam_tip: Altimeter = sealed aneroid capsule in a case connected to static. Static pressure drops in the climb, capsule expands, needle winds up. Subscale sets the reference pressure.
key_facts: Sealed aneroid capsule inside a static-pressurised case
Capsule expands as static pressure drops in the climb
Linkage converts expansion into altitude on the dial
Subscale (Kollsman window) biases the scale for QNH/QFE/QNE
Only uses static pressure, not pitot
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With respect to the altimeter: describe the use of the altimeter settings QNH, QFE and QNE
concept: Three subscale settings, three different reference datums. QNH is set so the altimeter reads altitude above mean sea level (ASL) — this is what you use for VFR cruise, most ATC instructions, and reading the altimeter against published elevations like an aerodrome altitude. Set current QNH and the altimeter should read the aerodrome elevation when you're parked. QFE is set so the altimeter reads height above a specific aerodrome (so you read zero when on the runway). It's less common in NZ but still used in some military and training environments. QNE is 1013 hPa (the ISA standard setting), used above the transition altitude for flight levels — every aircraft above transition altitude sets 1013 so they're all using the same reference, regardless of local pressure. NZ transition altitude is 13,000 ft.
exam_tip: QNH: altimeter reads ALT above mean sea level (most common). QFE: reads height above aerodrome (reads zero on runway). QNE: 1013 hPa for flight levels above transition altitude. NZ transition altitude is 13,000 ft.
key_facts: QNH: altitude above mean sea level
QFE: height above specific aerodrome (reads zero on runway)
QNE: 1013 hPa / ISA standard, used for flight levels
NZ transition altitude: 13,000 ft
Correct subscale is critical for vertical separation and terrain clearance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With respect to the altimeter: explain the effect of blockages and leaks, and the remedies available to the pilot
concept: The altimeter only cares about static pressure, so blocks and leaks affect it differently from the ASI. A blocked static line freezes the reading wherever the blockage happened — the altimeter just sits there even as you climb or descend. A leak in the static line (inside a pressurised cabin) lets cabin pressure reach the capsule, and since cabin altitude is usually lower than the real altitude, the altimeter over-reads. In an unpressurised aircraft, a leak usually means the altimeter reads close to normal but may lag or be slightly off. The remedy for a blocked static line is the alternate static source, which most aircraft have. When selected, it vents the static line to cabin air. Check the POH — cabin static is usually slightly lower than real ambient, so the altimeter may over-read by 20 to 50 ft once you switch to alternate static.
exam_tip: Blocked static line: altimeter freezes. Alternate static source is the remedy. Expect a small error (usually over-reading) when using it — check the POH for the correction.
key_facts: Blocked static: altimeter freezes at the pre-block reading
Leak in pressurised cabin: altimeter over-reads
Leak in unpressurised cabin: small or no error
Alternate static source vents to cabin air
POH lists the typical correction when using alternate static
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With respect to the altimeter: state the serviceability checks
concept: Altimeter serviceability checks start on the ground. Set the current QNH in the subscale and check that the altimeter reads the known aerodrome elevation within acceptable tolerance — most aircraft require within ±75 ft for IFR use. If it's way off, the instrument is unserviceable. Verify the subscale moves smoothly through its range. In flight, cross-check the altimeter against the GPS or known terrain as a sanity check. After landing, check again that current QNH gives the aerodrome elevation. Any significant drift means the instrument needs servicing. Part 91 also has periodic calibration requirements for IFR-certified altimeters.
exam_tip: On the ground with current QNH, altimeter should read aerodrome elevation within ±75 ft (IFR tolerance). Cross-check against GPS or terrain in flight.
key_facts: Set current QNH and verify altimeter reads aerodrome elevation
IFR tolerance typically ±75 ft
Subscale should move smoothly through its range
Cross-check against GPS or known terrain in flight
Periodic calibration required for IFR-certified altimeters
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the altimeter: instrument error
concept: Instrument error on the altimeter is the same idea as ASI instrument error — tolerances in the capsule, linkage friction, slight drift over time. It's small and usually stable across the altitude range. For IFR work the instrument is tested periodically and any excessive error makes it unserviceable. You can't correct it in flight; you just verify within tolerance before each flight.
exam_tip: Instrument error: mechanical tolerances in the altimeter. Small, steady. Verified within tolerance before each flight.
key_facts: Mechanical tolerances and linkage friction
Small and relatively constant
Cannot be corrected in flight
Verified by comparing to aerodrome elevation
Periodic certification required for IFR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the altimeter: position error
concept: Position error on the altimeter comes from the static ports sitting in airflow that isn't quite free-stream. Different speeds and angles of attack produce slightly different local pressures at the port, and the altimeter picks up the error. The POH accounts for it in the IAS/CAS correction (same chart as the ASI). Position error on the altimeter is usually small at normal cruise speeds but can be meaningful at slow flight with high angle of attack.
exam_tip: Position error: local airflow at the static port isn't quite ambient. Small at cruise, larger at slow flight / high AoA. Same origin as ASI position error.
key_facts: Local airflow at the static port differs slightly from free-stream
Varies with airspeed, configuration and angle of attack
Small at normal cruise
Larger at slow flight / high AoA
Shares origin with ASI position error
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the altimeter: lag
concept: Lag is the altimeter's delayed response when the rate of pressure change is rapid — a high rate of climb or descent. The capsule takes a moment to fully respond to the new static pressure, and the mechanical linkage adds its own delay. At a steady 500 fpm on a Cessna 172 it's barely noticeable. At a steep descent of 2,000 fpm or more, the altimeter can lag behind the real altitude by a measurable amount. In practice it means that if you're descending hard to cross a waypoint at a given altitude, ease the descent a bit early to let the altimeter catch up — especially important on instrument approaches.
exam_tip: Lag: altimeter takes time to respond to rapid pressure changes. Worst in steep descents/climbs. Ease off early to let it catch up.
key_facts: Delayed response to rapid pressure change
Caused by capsule response time and linkage delay
Small at normal rates
Significant at 2,000+ fpm
Ease off early in steep descents to let the altimeter catch up
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the altimeter: temperature error
concept: Temperature error happens because the altimeter assumes ISA temperature when it converts pressure to altitude. On a cold day, the air column below you is denser than ISA, so a given pressure drop corresponds to less altitude gained than the instrument thinks — your true altitude is lower than indicated. On a hot day, it's the opposite: the altimeter over-states altitude loss, so your true altitude is higher than indicated. The rule pilots care about is the cold-weather one, because it's the dangerous direction: 'from high to low (temperature or pressure), look out below'. Cold temperatures mean you're lower than the altimeter says, which matters for terrain clearance. NZ winter IFR ops in the South Island routinely need temperature correction on approach charts — AIP NZ publishes the correction tables.
exam_tip: Cold = lower than indicated (dangerous for terrain clearance). Hot = higher than indicated. 'High to low, look out below.' Correction tables in AIP NZ for cold-weather IFR approaches.
key_facts: Altimeter assumes ISA temperature for conversion
Cold air: true altitude LOWER than indicated
Hot air: true altitude HIGHER than indicated
'High to low, look out below' — applies to temperature and pressure
AIP NZ publishes cold-temp approach corrections
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the altimeter: an incorrectly set altimeter subscale setting
concept: Set the wrong subscale and you get the wrong altitude — full stop. The rule of thumb is 1 hPa equals about 30 ft. So if the real QNH is 1013 and you've got 1003 in the window, you'll over-read by about 300 ft (you'll think you're 300 ft higher than you really are). Going from high to low pressure without updating the subscale means your true altitude is LOWER than indicated — the classic setup for CFIT on a descent into an unfamiliar area. 'From high to low, look out below' again. Always set the current QNH on taxi, update on climb through the transition altitude (to 1013), and set the new aerodrome QNH on descent through the transition level.
exam_tip: 1 hPa ≈ 30 ft. Going from high to low pressure without resetting: true alt LOWER than indicated. Always update subscale at transition altitude/level.
key_facts: 1 hPa ≈ 30 ft altitude error
Going from high to low pressure without resetting: true alt LOWER
'High to low, look out below' — applies to pressure and temperature
Set QNH on taxi, 1013 climbing through transition altitude
Set new QNH descending through transition level
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With respect to the VSI: explain the basic principle of operation
concept: The VSI measures rate of climb or descent by comparing the current static pressure with the static pressure a few seconds ago. Inside the instrument there's a capsule connected directly to the static line, sitting in a case that's also connected to static but through a restricted leak (a calibrated orifice). When you climb, static pressure drops fast in the capsule and slowly in the case, so the capsule contracts relative to the case and a linkage shows a rate of climb. When you descend, it's the opposite. Level off and both sides equalise and the needle returns to zero. The calibrated leak is what gives the VSI its characteristic lag.
exam_tip: VSI compares current static pressure against a delayed version of it, via a calibrated leak. Capsule + case both static, but one delayed. Lag is built in.
key_facts: Measures rate of change of static pressure
Capsule connects directly to static line
Case connects to static via calibrated leak (orifice)
Pressure difference between capsule and case drives the needle
Calibrated leak is the source of lag
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With respect to the VSI: explain the effect of blockages and leaks
concept: Blockage or leakage in the VSI line affects it the same way blockages affect the altimeter, but the VSI is even fussier because it's comparing two pressures across a calibrated leak. A blocked static line freezes the VSI at zero (no rate because both sides are locked at the same pressure). A leak from a pressurised cabin can cause the VSI to show a false rate of descent (or a stuck rate). In an unpressurised trainer, a leak in the static line is usually less dramatic but can cause erratic or lagging indications. The alternate static source is the cockpit remedy; when selected, the VSI should return to behaving roughly normally.
exam_tip: Blocked static: VSI reads zero. Leak in pressurised cabin: false rate. Alternate static is the fix.
key_facts: Blocked static line: VSI reads zero
Leak in pressurised cabin: false rate or stuck reading
Leak in unpressurised aircraft: erratic or lagging indication
Alternate static source restores approximately normal operation
VSI is the most sensitive of the three to static issues
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: With respect to the VSI: state the serviceability checks
concept: VSI serviceability checks are simple. On the ground with the aircraft stationary, the VSI should read zero — if it's hung up off zero, note the error or have it adjusted (some VSIs have a small zero-set screw). In flight, cross-check the VSI against altimeter change over time and against the attitude indicator. A climb should show a positive rate roughly matching what the altimeter is doing. A stuck VSI or one that's hung up with a false bias is still usable if you know about it, but it's one of the least reliable instruments in the panel for that reason.
exam_tip: On the ground, VSI should read zero. If not, note the error or zero-set. In flight, cross-check against altimeter rate of change.
key_facts: Should read zero on the ground
Zero-set screw on many instruments
Cross-check against altimeter rate in flight
Compare with attitude indicator for consistency
Instant vertical speed indicators (IVSI) reduce lag
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the VSI: position error
concept: Position error on the VSI comes from the same source as position error on the altimeter and ASI — the static port picking up local airflow that isn't quite free-stream. At different speeds and angles of attack the port sees a slightly wrong pressure, and the VSI responds to the change in that slightly wrong pressure. In practice it shows up as small steady offsets or mild over/under-reading during transitions in configuration (flap selection, for example). It's usually small compared to lag.
exam_tip: VSI position error: local airflow at the static port isn't quite free-stream. Usually small. Shows up during configuration changes.
key_facts: Same origin as altimeter/ASI position error
Local static port airflow not quite free-stream
Varies with speed, AoA, configuration
Usually small
Most noticeable during flap selection or similar transients
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the VSI: lag
concept: Lag is the VSI's defining characteristic. The calibrated leak that makes the instrument work also means it takes a few seconds for the capsule and case pressures to settle after you change pitch attitude. You establish a climb, the needle takes 3 to 9 seconds to catch up and show the actual rate. Level off, and the needle takes a similar time to return to zero. On a typical non-instantaneous VSI, don't chase the needle — establish the pitch attitude for the desired climb or descent, then let the VSI confirm the rate. Modern IVSIs (instantaneous vertical speed indicators) use an accelerometer to reduce lag, but basic trainers still have the classic lag-prone instrument.
exam_tip: Lag: 3-9 seconds on a basic VSI. Don't chase the needle — set pitch attitude first, then let the VSI confirm. IVSIs reduce lag using an accelerometer.
key_facts: Inherent to the calibrated leak design
Typical lag 3-9 seconds on basic VSIs
Appears when entering or leaving a climb/descent
Don't chase the needle — set attitude first
IVSIs reduce lag with an accelerometer input
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Aerodynamics and Systems, AIP NZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Describe the gyroscopic principles of rigidity and precession
concept: A gyroscope is a spinning mass, and spinning masses do two odd but useful things. The first is rigidity in space: once you've got a wheel spinning fast, it wants to keep its spin axis pointing in the same direction relative to the universe, not relative to you or your aeroplane. Tilt the frame around it and the wheel just sits there, stubbornly pointing where it was. That's how your attitude indicator and HI/DI know which way is up and which way is north even while you're banking and turning. The second property is precession. If you apply a force to the rim of a spinning gyro, the reaction doesn't show up where you pushed — it shows up 90 degrees further around the wheel in the direction of rotation. Sounds weird, and it is, but it's what makes a turn indicator work: yaw the aeroplane and the gyro reacts by tilting, and that tilt drives the needle.
exam_tip: Two properties, two jobs. Rigidity keeps AIs and HIs stable; precession makes turn indicators work. Precession is 90 degrees ahead in the direction of rotation.
key_facts: Rigidity in space: a spinning gyro resists any change in the direction of its spin axis
Precession: a force applied to the rim acts 90 degrees further on in the direction of spin
Rigidity depends on rotor mass, mass distribution (concentrated at the rim) and RPM
Rigidity is used by the AI and HI/DI; precession is used by the turn indicator/turn coordinator
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Describe a typical pneumatic system for powering gyro instruments
concept: In a pneumatic (vacuum/suction) system, an engine-driven vacuum pump sucks air out of the instrument cases. Filtered cabin air rushes in through a small nozzle aimed at buckets machined into the rim of the gyro rotor, spinning it up to around 10,000–18,000 RPM. The air then exits through the pump and overboard. A vacuum regulator holds the suction to the right value (typically around 4.5 to 5.5 in Hg for the AI and HI), and a suction gauge on the panel lets you check it. Simple, cheap, no electrical load — but it's only as good as the vacuum pump, the filter and the regulator, and you can't drive a turn indicator at high altitude reliably because the air gets too thin to spin the rotor fast enough.
exam_tip: Vacuum pump + filtered air + jet on gyro rotor + regulator + suction gauge. Know the rough suction range and that the AI and HI are the instruments usually driven this way.
key_facts: Engine-driven vacuum pump creates suction in the instrument cases
Filtered air is drawn in through a nozzle that spins the gyro rotor
A regulator maintains the suction at around 4.5–5.5 in Hg
Typical gyro RPM in a vacuum system is around 10,000–18,000
The suction gauge on the panel is the pilot's only indication that the system is working
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: List the advantages of an electrically powered gyro system
concept: An electric gyro spins up much faster (often 20,000+ RPM), which gives you more rigidity and a more stable instrument. It doesn't care about altitude — no thin-air problem — and it doesn't care whether the engine is turning, so it'll keep working if the donkey quits. You can also run it through an inverter or off the aeroplane bus, which gives you a second power source for redundancy. The filter and vacuum pump, which are both wear items in a vacuum system, are gone. Downsides? Higher electrical load and slightly more complexity, but on any serious IFR machine the electric gyro wins.
exam_tip: Learn the list: higher RPM (more rigid), works at altitude, works with engine failure, no filters/pump to clog or fail, can be fed from multiple electrical sources.
key_facts: Higher rotor RPM gives greater rigidity and more accurate indications
Works at any altitude — unaffected by thin air
Continues to operate after engine failure if electrical power is maintained
Eliminates the vacuum pump and air filter (both failure items)
Can be powered from multiple electrical sources for redundancy
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Describe: real wander
concept: Real wander is when the gyro physically drifts off its correct alignment because of something mechanical — friction in the bearings, rotor imbalance, worn gimbals, or an uneven force inside the instrument. The gyro's spin axis is actually moving relative to space. That's the 'real' part: it's a genuine mechanical error, and it builds up steadily over time. In an old, tired vacuum HI you'll notice you're resetting it to the compass every 10–15 minutes because of this.
exam_tip: Real wander = physical/mechanical cause. The gyro actually moves in space. Remember: real = mechanical fault.
key_facts: Caused by mechanical factors: bearing friction, imbalance, worn gimbals
The gyro's spin axis genuinely moves relative to space
Gets worse as the instrument ages
Corrected by periodic realignment to the magnetic compass in the HI/DI
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Describe: apparent wander
concept: Apparent wander is when the gyro hasn't actually moved at all — the Earth has moved underneath it. Because a gyro holds its axis rigid in space, and because the Earth is spinning on its tilted axis and you're flying across its curved surface, the relationship between the gyro and the local 'north' or 'horizontal' is constantly changing. The gyro hasn't wandered; your reference has. There are two flavours: Earth rate (caused by Earth's rotation, worst at the poles, zero at the equator) and transport wander (caused by you physically moving across the curved surface of the Earth).
exam_tip: Apparent wander = gyro is fine, your reference has moved. Two causes: Earth rate (rotation) and transport wander (your motion).
key_facts: The gyro has not actually moved — the Earth has
Earth rate is caused by the rotation of the Earth; maximum at the poles, zero at the equator
Transport wander is caused by your movement across the curved Earth
At mid-latitudes, Earth rate is around 11–12 degrees per hour
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between real and apparent wander
concept: Here's the simple test. Real wander means the gyro itself is actually moving in space because of a mechanical problem — friction, wear, imbalance. Apparent wander means the gyro is perfectly stable, but your frame of reference (the Earth's surface and its 'north') is moving. If you parked a perfect gyro in a hangar overnight pointing at a star, real wander is zero but apparent wander would still creep in because the Earth rotated beneath it. Both show up as an HI that's drifted off the compass — which is why you reset it against the wet compass periodically in straight and level flight.
exam_tip: Classic exam question: 'differentiate between'. Answer in one line each — real = mechanical (gyro moves), apparent = Earth/transport (reference moves). Both need correction, neither is avoidable.
key_facts: Real wander: the gyro physically moves due to mechanical faults
Apparent wander: the gyro is stable but the Earth reference has moved
Real wander gets worse with age; apparent wander is predictable and depends on latitude and aircraft motion
Both are corrected by resetting the HI to the magnetic compass every 10–15 minutes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: With respect to the turn indicator/turn coordinator: describe the basic principle of operation
concept: The turn indicator uses a rate gyro. The rotor spins in a fixed horizontal axis, mounted in a single gimbal that's spring-restrained. When you yaw the aeroplane, the gyro tries to hold its position in space — but because of precession, the yawing force applied through the gimbal makes the gyro tilt. The amount it tilts is proportional to the rate of turn, and that tilt moves the needle (or the little aeroplane symbol in a turn coordinator). In a standard turn indicator it measures yaw only; in a turn coordinator the gimbal is canted about 30 degrees upward, so it senses both roll and yaw, which is why the turn coordinator responds sooner when you bank.
exam_tip: Rate gyro, single gimbal, spring-restrained. Precession is what moves the needle. Turn coordinator gimbal is canted ~30 deg so it also senses roll.
key_facts: It's a rate gyro — tilt is proportional to rate of turn
Single spring-restrained gimbal
Precession converts yaw into gimbal tilt, which drives the needle
Turn coordinator has a gimbal canted about 30 degrees so it senses roll as well as yaw
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: With respect to the turn indicator/turn coordinator: state the serviceability checks
concept: Serviceability checks for the turn indicator/turn coordinator are simple and done every flight. Before taxi, the suction or electrical source should be showing normal (so the gyro has power), and the needle/miniature aeroplane should be erect and centred and the ball in the middle when you're stationary. Taxiing gives you a free test: a left taxi turn should move the needle/wingtip left and the ball should skid to the right; a right taxi turn should move it right and the ball should skid left. Needle and ball move in opposite directions in a skidding taxi turn. If the instrument doesn't respond, or responds the wrong way, you're not going flying.
exam_tip: The taxi check is the classic: turn left, needle goes left, ball goes right. Works every time — don't miss it on the ground.
key_facts: Before start/taxi: instrument should be erect and ball centred
Taxi left: needle/wingtip goes left, ball skids right
Taxi right: needle/wingtip goes right, ball skids left
Confirms both the gyro is spun up and the needle is free to move
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the turn indicator/turn coordinator: suction error
concept: Suction error applies to vacuum-driven turn indicators. If the suction is too low, the gyro isn't spinning fast enough, so it's less rigid and its reaction to precession is weaker — the needle under-reads the rate of turn. If suction is too high, the gyro over-spins and the opposite happens: the needle over-reads. Either way, your 'rate 1' might not be rate 1 at all. This is why the suction gauge matters and why vacuum-driven turn indicators are rare on modern IFR aeroplanes — electric gyros don't have this problem.
exam_tip: Low suction = under-reads the turn. High suction = over-reads. Either way, the rate you're flying isn't the rate shown.
key_facts: Affects vacuum-driven turn indicators
Low suction: gyro spins slow, instrument under-reads
High suction: gyro spins fast, instrument over-reads
Check the suction gauge before relying on the turn indicator
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the turn indicator/turn coordinator: yaw with pitch error
concept: If you pitch the nose up or down during a turn, the gimbal in a turn indicator/turn coordinator can sense that pitch change as a yaw input, because the gyro axis isn't perfectly aligned anymore. The result is that aggressive pitch changes during turns — especially steep turns with significant nose-up or nose-down attitude — cause the needle to twitch or wander. Smooth flying minimises it; the error is small in normal IFR handling but shows up in abrupt manoeuvring.
exam_tip: Pitch during a turn corrupts the turn indication slightly because of how the gimbal senses motion. Fly smoothly and it's not a problem.
key_facts: Pitch inputs during a turn cause small errors in turn rate indication
Caused by the gimbal geometry — the gyro can't perfectly separate pitch from yaw
Worst during aggressive manoeuvring, negligible in smooth IFR flying
Smooth, coordinated flight minimises the error
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: With respect to the heading/direction indicator (HI/DI): describe the basic principle of operation
concept: The HI/DI uses a gyro with its spin axis horizontal, mounted in two gimbals so it's free to move in all three axes. Because of rigidity in space, the gyro holds its alignment while the aeroplane turns around it. A card graduated in degrees is geared to the gyro, so as you turn, the card rotates against a fixed lubber line and you read off the heading. The gyro itself doesn't know which way is north — you have to tell it, by caging the instrument on the ground or in straight and level flight and aligning the card with the magnetic compass. After that, it just holds that reference until wander makes it drift enough that you need to reset it again.
exam_tip: Horizontal-axis gyro, two gimbals, rigidity holds the reference, card geared to the gyro. You must set it from the compass — it has no idea where north is on its own.
key_facts: Horizontal spin axis, double gimbal mounting
Uses rigidity in space to hold the heading reference
Card is mechanically geared to the gyro and reads against a fixed lubber line
Must be manually aligned to the magnetic compass before use and reset periodically
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: With respect to the HI/DI: state the advantages of an electrically driven HI/DI
concept: Same benefits as any electric gyro — only they matter more here because the HI is what you're steering by in IMC. Higher RPM means greater rigidity, which means less real wander. It works unaffected by altitude, so it's reliable at your cruise level. It keeps running if the engine quits (as long as you've got electrics), which matters when you're flying partial panel. And it can be driven from a second bus or an inverter for redundancy. Most modern IFR machines run the HI electrically for all these reasons.
exam_tip: Same list as 56.4.6 but applied to the HI specifically: more rigidity, altitude independent, works after engine failure, redundant power options.
key_facts: Higher rotor RPM gives more rigidity and less drift
Unaffected by altitude — works at IFR cruise levels
Keeps running on battery/alternate power after engine failure
No vacuum filter or pump to fail
Can be powered from a secondary electrical source for redundancy
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: With respect to the HI/DI: state the serviceability checks
concept: Check it's powered (suction or electrical source showing normal), check it's uncaged, and align it to the magnetic compass in straight and level unaccelerated flight. On the ground before taxi, set it to the runway heading or the compass heading — this gives you a known starting point. On a long taxi and during every straight and level segment in flight, cross-check against the wet compass and realign if it's drifted. Any sticking, erratic movement, or failure to hold a heading means the instrument is unserviceable.
exam_tip: Set it in S&L unaccelerated flight from the magnetic compass. Check every 10–15 minutes. Any unusual behaviour = unserviceable.
key_facts: Confirm power (suction or electrical) is normal
Ensure instrument is uncaged
Align with magnetic compass in straight and level unaccelerated flight
Cross-check and reset every 10–15 minutes in cruise
Any sticking or erratic behaviour means it's unserviceable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the HI/DI: gimbal error
concept: Gimbal error shows up when the aeroplane is banked and pitched at the same time. The geometry of the gimbals means the heading reading can be slightly wrong during a turn — the card doesn't rotate linearly with aircraft heading if you're also pitched up or down. In straight and level flight it's zero, but in a steep turn you may see a few degrees of error. It self-corrects when you roll wings level, so it's not usually a concern — just don't try to nail a heading mid-turn.
exam_tip: Gimbal error appears only when banked AND pitched. Disappears when wings level. Small and self-correcting.
key_facts: Caused by the geometry of the gimbal mounts when pitched and banked simultaneously
Zero in straight and level flight
Noticeable during steep or manoeuvring turns
Self-corrects once wings are level
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the HI/DI: real drift
concept: Real drift in the HI/DI is the same thing as real wander: the gyro itself is physically moving because of mechanical factors — bearing friction, rotor imbalance, worn gimbals. It builds up steadily and is why you have to reset the HI against the compass periodically. An older, tired vacuum HI can drift a few degrees in 10 minutes; a good electric one is much better. If it's drifting more than a couple of degrees per 15 minutes, get it looked at.
exam_tip: Real drift = mechanical wear inside the instrument. Cured by resetting, eventually by overhauling.
key_facts: Caused by bearing friction, imbalance and wear inside the instrument
Cumulative — gets worse over time
Corrected by periodic resetting against the magnetic compass
Excessive drift is grounds to have the instrument serviced
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the HI/DI: apparent drift/wander
concept: Apparent drift in the HI is the same apparent wander as before, applied to heading. The gyro is perfectly rigid in space, but as the Earth rotates (Earth rate) and as you fly across the curved surface (transport wander), the 'north' you care about is moving underneath the gyro. At mid-latitudes the Earth rate component is around 11–12 degrees per hour. On a long leg across the country you'd see the HI drift a noticeable amount purely from this, with nothing wrong mechanically. That's why the resetting habit is non-negotiable.
exam_tip: Apparent drift = Earth rate + transport wander. The gyro is fine, north is moving. Around 11–12 deg/hr at NZ latitudes from Earth rate alone.
key_facts: Apparent wander applied to heading
Earth rate component is around 11–12 deg/hour at NZ latitudes
Transport wander is the additional effect of your motion across the curved Earth
Not a mechanical fault — it's geometry
Corrected by resetting the HI to the magnetic compass every 10–15 minutes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: With respect to the attitude indicator (AI): describe the basic principle of operation (for both air-driven and electrically driven instruments)
concept: The AI uses a vertical-axis gyro in a double gimbal, with the spin axis pointing straight up. Because of rigidity, the gyro holds its vertical reference while the aeroplane pitches and rolls around it. A horizon bar is attached to the outer gimbal, and what you see on the face of the instrument is the aeroplane symbol pitching and rolling against that fixed horizon. In an air-driven AI, suction pulls filtered air past the rotor buckets to spin it. In an electrically driven AI, an AC motor or DC motor spins the rotor — faster, more rigid, and more reliable at altitude. Either way, a pendulous vane mechanism or an electrically driven erection system keeps the gyro's axis pointing true vertical to compensate for precession over time.
exam_tip: Vertical spin axis, double gimbal, rigidity holds the horizon reference. Erection system keeps it aligned with true vertical. Electric = faster, more rigid, altitude-independent.
key_facts: Vertical spin axis in a double gimbal
Rigidity in space provides the horizon reference
Air-driven versions use suction and filtered air through rotor buckets
Electric versions use AC or DC motors for much higher RPM
An erection mechanism (pendulous vanes or electrical) keeps the gyro axis vertical
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: With respect to the AI: state the typical limits in freedom
concept: Typical AI gimbal freedom is limited: around 110 degrees of pitch either side and 110 degrees of roll either side in modern designs, but older aeroplanes (particularly air-driven AIs in training machines) can have much tighter limits — often only around 60 degrees in pitch and 100 in roll. Exceed those limits and the gyro 'tumbles' — it flips violently and the instrument becomes useless until you cage it or let the erection system bring it back. That's why aerobatic and upset training is done on an aeroplane with either an AI that can handle the attitudes or none at all.
exam_tip: Air-driven training AI: roughly 60 deg pitch, 100 deg roll before it tumbles. Modern electric AIs are much higher. Exceed the limits and it tumbles.
key_facts: Typical older air-driven AI: around 60 degrees pitch freedom, 100 degrees roll freedom
Modern electric AIs may have up to around 110 degrees either way in both axes
Exceeding the limits causes the gyro to tumble
A tumbled AI must be recaged or allowed to self-erect before it can be used again
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the AI: pendulosity error
concept: Pendulosity error comes from the erection mechanism itself. The pendulous vanes hanging under the gyro sense gravity to keep the spin axis vertical — but gravity isn't the only force acting on a pendulum. In any sustained turn, the apparent vertical is tilted because of centripetal acceleration, and the vanes feel that tilted 'vertical' as if it were real. The result is that the gyro slowly erects toward a false vertical during a prolonged turn, and when you roll out you'll see a small bank indication that isn't really there. It corrects itself within a minute or two of straight flight.
exam_tip: Pendulosity/erection mechanism is fooled by sustained turns. After rollout, the AI shows a false small bank for a minute or so.
key_facts: Caused by the pendulous vane erection system responding to apparent vertical
Most noticeable after a prolonged turn
Shows up as a small bank indication after rolling wings level
Self-corrects within one to two minutes of straight and level flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the AI: erection error
concept: Erection error is closely related: any acceleration or deceleration the erection mechanism senses as 'vertical' will nudge the gyro toward a false vertical. On the takeoff roll, the vanes feel the acceleration and interpret it as a forward tilt — so the erection system tries to push the gyro's axis backward, giving a small nose-up indication. Prolonged sideslip, taxi turns, and any other sustained non-vertical acceleration will have similar effects. The cure is the same: in straight and level unaccelerated flight the erection system corrects it.
exam_tip: Any sustained acceleration confuses the erection mechanism. Takeoff = false nose-up. It recovers in S&L unaccelerated flight.
key_facts: Caused by the erection system interpreting acceleration as a tilt of the vertical
Takeoff acceleration causes a false nose-up indication
Also shows up during taxi turns and sideslips
Corrects itself in straight and level unaccelerated flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the AI: acceleration error
concept: Acceleration error is what you actually notice at the start of the takeoff roll. As the aeroplane accelerates, the pendulous vanes are thrown back by inertia (the same way your head is pushed back into the seat), so the erection mechanism is fooled into thinking the nose is pitching up. It tries to correct, which tilts the gyro slightly — and the horizon bar shows a small climb that you aren't actually making, plus a small right bank (in a left-handed engine installation, because of combined torque effects and the particular way the erection system responds). Deceleration gives you the opposite: a false nose-down and slight bank the other way.
exam_tip: Acceleration = false climb and slight bank. Deceleration = false descent and slight bank the other way. Common on the takeoff roll in a training aeroplane.
key_facts: Caused by inertia acting on the pendulous erection vanes
Acceleration: false climb indication plus a slight apparent bank
Deceleration: false descent indication plus a slight apparent bank
Noticeable on the takeoff roll in air-driven AIs, negligible in modern electric AIs
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting the AI: turning error
concept: Turning error in the AI is essentially the same pendulosity problem — centripetal acceleration in a turn tricks the erection mechanism into seeing the tilted apparent vertical as the real one. During the turn, the gyro slowly erects toward that false vertical, and when you roll out wings level, the AI shows a small bank in the opposite direction of the turn plus a slight pitch error. The faster and longer the turn, the bigger the error, but it's small and short-lived — a minute or two of S&L flight and it's gone. Electrically driven AIs with better erection systems minimise it.
exam_tip: After a sustained turn, the AI briefly shows a small opposite bank and pitch error. Self-corrects in S&L. Worse in air-driven instruments.
key_facts: Caused by the erection mechanism responding to centripetal acceleration during the turn
After rollout: shows a small bank opposite to the turn and a small pitch error
Magnitude increases with turn rate and duration
Self-corrects within one to two minutes of straight and level flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (FAA-H-8083-25); standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: With respect to remote-indicating compasses: describe the compass card presentation on the radio magnetic indicator (RMI)
concept: A remote-indicating compass takes the magnetic sensing job and moves it away from all the iron and electrical noise in the cockpit. A detector unit (the flux valve or flux gate) lives out in a wingtip or the tail where magnetic interference is minimal, and it feeds a signal to the RMI in the panel. On the face of an RMI you see a compass card that rotates against a fixed lubber line at the top — the number under the lubber line is your current magnetic heading. It looks just like a conventional HI, but the card is being driven electrically from the remote flux detector, not from a local gyro fighting with cabin iron. Most RMIs also carry one or two needles that point at selected NDB or VOR bearings, laid over the rotating compass card — that's why the RMI is so useful for IFR navigation.
exam_tip: Rotating compass card, fixed lubber line at the top, driven by a remote flux detector. Often carries ADF/VOR bearing pointers on the same face.
key_facts: The sensing element (flux valve) is mounted away from cockpit magnetic interference
Signals are fed electrically to the RMI in the panel
The compass card rotates against a fixed lubber line — the number under it is the current magnetic heading
Often overlaid with ADF and/or VOR bearing pointers for navigation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: With respect to remote-indicating compasses: describe interpretation of the annunciator, and the operation of the compass synchronising knob of the RMI
concept: The annunciator is that tiny black-and-white or cross-and-dot window on the RMI face that tells you whether the system is synchronised or not. When it's swinging back and forth between the two symbols, the flux detector and the compass card are aligned and everything's happy. If it sits steady on one symbol, the system has drifted and needs a manual resync. The synchronising knob is how you do it — press and rotate it in the indicated direction until the annunciator starts moving again. You'll need to resync after any big heading change sequence, after turning the master switch on, and occasionally in cruise if you notice the annunciator has stopped moving.
exam_tip: Annunciator oscillating = synced. Stopped on one side = out of sync. Press and turn the sync knob in the direction shown until oscillation returns.
key_facts: Annunciator oscillation means the system is synchronised
Annunciator stuck on one symbol means the system needs resyncing
Press and rotate the sync knob in the direction the annunciator is showing
Resync after power-up, after any large heading change, and periodically in cruise
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: With respect to remote-indicating compasses: explain the errors which can affect remote indicating compasses (including deviation), and how these can be avoided or reduced
concept: Remote-indicating compasses still suffer some of the same errors as a wet compass, just less of them. Deviation (from nearby iron and electrical fields) is minimised by mounting the flux detector well away from the cockpit, but it's still worth checking on a compass swing. Acceleration and turning errors — the classic wet compass errors caused by the magnet's dip and pendulous mounting — are largely eliminated because the flux detector has no pendulous magnet; it senses the Earth's magnetic field directly via coils. You can still get small errors during violent manoeuvring because the detector itself can tilt, but in normal IFR flying it's much steadier than a wet compass. Avoid or reduce the remaining errors by keeping loose iron and electronic devices away from the detector area, and by checking the compass swing regularly.
exam_tip: Deviation is reduced (not eliminated) by remote mounting. Acceleration and turning errors are mostly gone because there's no pendulous magnet. Still needs a compass swing.
key_facts: Deviation is reduced by mounting the flux valve away from cockpit iron and electrics
Acceleration and turning errors are largely eliminated — no pendulous magnet assembly
Small errors remain during steep manoeuvring because the detector itself can tilt
Regular compass swings are still required to check residual deviation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; standard IFR instrument theory

syllabus_desc: With respect to radio waves define: cycle
concept: A cycle is one complete oscillation of a radio wave — from zero up to peak, back down through zero to the opposite peak, and back to zero again. Think of it as one complete wiggle. String a lot of these cycles together in a second and you've got a radio wave. Everything in radio comes back to this: how many cycles per second, how long each cycle is in space, and how they behave on the way from the transmitter to your aeroplane.
exam_tip: One cycle = one full wave — zero, peak, zero, trough, zero. Nothing more exciting than that.
key_facts: A cycle is one complete oscillation of a radio wave
Starts at zero, rises to peak, falls through zero to trough, returns to zero
The building block of all frequency and wavelength measurements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; standard radio theory texts

syllabus_desc: With respect to radio waves define: frequency, and state the unit describing frequency
concept: Frequency is the number of complete cycles happening every second. More cycles per second = higher frequency. It's measured in hertz (Hz), where 1 Hz = 1 cycle per second. Aviation radios deal in much bigger numbers, so you'll almost always see kHz (thousands of cycles per second), MHz (millions), or GHz (billions). Your VHF comms live around 118–137 MHz, your VOR around 108–118 MHz, and your GNSS signals up around 1.2–1.6 GHz.
exam_tip: Frequency = cycles per second, measured in hertz (Hz). Know the prefixes: kHz, MHz, GHz.
key_facts: Frequency = number of cycles per second
Unit: hertz (Hz); 1 Hz = 1 cycle per second
kHz = thousand, MHz = million, GHz = billion cycles per second
VHF comms: around 118–137 MHz; VOR: 108–118 MHz; GNSS: ~1.2–1.6 GHz
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; standard radio theory texts

syllabus_desc: With respect to radio waves define: wavelength and explain how it is related to frequency
concept: Wavelength is the physical length of one complete cycle as it travels through space — measured in metres. Because all radio waves travel at the speed of light (roughly 300,000,000 metres per second), wavelength and frequency are locked together by a simple formula: wavelength (m) = 300 / frequency (MHz). Higher frequency = shorter wavelength, and vice versa. A 120 MHz VHF signal has a wavelength of about 2.5 m. A 1.2 GHz GNSS signal has a wavelength of about 0.25 m — which is why GPS antennas are so small.
exam_tip: Wavelength (m) = 300 / frequency (MHz). High freq = short wavelength. Know the formula — examiners ask it directly.
key_facts: Wavelength = the physical length of one cycle, measured in metres
All radio waves travel at the speed of light (≈ 300,000,000 m/s)
Formula: wavelength (m) = 300 / frequency (MHz)
Higher frequency means shorter wavelength
Example: 120 MHz → 2.5 m wavelength
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; standard radio theory texts

syllabus_desc: Describe the propagation of direct waves and the rule of thumb formula for calculation range of reception
concept: Direct (or line-of-sight) waves are the VHF and higher-frequency signals that travel in a straight line from the transmitter to you. They don't bend around the Earth, they don't bounce off the ionosphere — if the antenna can't 'see' yours, you're not getting the signal. That means range depends almost entirely on height: the higher you are, the further over the horizon you can see the ground-based antenna. The rule-of-thumb formula is: range (nm) = 1.23 × (√h₁ + √h₂), where h₁ and h₂ are the heights in feet of the two antennas. For a VHF COMS station at 100 ft talking to you at 10,000 ft, that's about 1.23 × (10 + 100) = 135 nm. Simple, and it matches reality surprisingly well.
exam_tip: Direct waves = line-of-sight. Range (nm) = 1.23 × (√h₁ + √h₂). Heights in feet. Memorise the formula — it's an exam favourite.
key_facts: Direct waves travel in straight lines from transmitter to receiver
Used at VHF and higher frequencies
Range is limited by line-of-sight — depends on the heights of both antennas
Rule-of-thumb: range (nm) = 1.23 × (√h₁ + √h₂), with heights in feet
Used by VHF comms, VOR, DME, ILS, GNSS and most other modern aviation nav/comms
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ; standard radio theory texts

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of PSR, including the principles of radar ranging and direction
concept: Primary Surveillance Radar (PSR) works by shouting and listening. The radar transmits a short, high-energy pulse of radio energy from a rotating directional antenna, and then listens for the reflection bouncing back off anything solid out there — an aeroplane, a mountain, a rain cloud. Ranging is dead simple: the pulse travels at the speed of light, so by measuring how long it takes for the echo to return, the radar knows exactly how far away the target is. Direction is equally simple: the antenna is highly directional and its bearing is known, so whichever way it's pointing when the echo comes back is the target's bearing. Combine range and bearing and you've got a position — no help required from the aeroplane at all. That's the beauty and the limitation of PSR: it sees everything, but it can't tell you who's who.
exam_tip: PSR: transmit pulse, time the echo for range, use antenna bearing for direction. No aircraft cooperation needed, but no ID either.
key_facts: Transmits short high-energy pulses from a rotating directional antenna
Listens for reflected energy from any solid object in the beam
Range is calculated from the time taken for the echo to return (speed of light)
Bearing is taken from the direction the antenna is pointing
No cooperation required from the target — but also no identification
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; standard radar theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the operational range of PSR: pulse repetition frequency (PRF)
concept: Pulse Repetition Frequency is the number of pulses the radar fires every second. Between each pulse, the radar has to wait long enough for the most distant echo to come back before firing the next one — otherwise it can't tell which pulse produced which echo. So lower PRF gives longer maximum range (more listening time between pulses), and higher PRF gives shorter maximum range but more updates per sweep. It's a straightforward trade-off: range versus update rate.
exam_tip: Lower PRF = longer maximum range. Higher PRF = shorter range but faster updates.
key_facts: PRF is the number of pulses transmitted per second
Between pulses, the radar must wait long enough for the furthest echo to return
Lower PRF → longer maximum range
Higher PRF → shorter maximum range but more frequent target updates
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; standard radar theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the operational range of PSR: pulse width (PW)
concept: Pulse width is how long each individual pulse of energy lasts — typically measured in microseconds. A wider pulse puts more energy on the target, which means a stronger return and therefore longer effective range, especially against small or distant targets. But the downside is resolution: two targets closer together than the length of the pulse can't be separated — they blur into one blob. So a long pulse sees further but discriminates less; a short pulse sees less far but separates targets better.
exam_tip: Wider pulse = more energy, longer range, worse resolution. Narrower pulse = less range, better resolution.
key_facts: Pulse width = the duration of each transmitted pulse
Longer pulse delivers more energy and gives longer detection range
Longer pulse reduces range resolution — targets close together blur together
Shorter pulse improves resolution but reduces maximum range
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; standard radar theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the operational range of PSR: antenna rate of rotation
concept: The antenna rate of rotation determines how often the beam sweeps over any given target. Slower rotation means each target is illuminated for longer and more pulses hit it, which increases the chance of detection and improves the quality of the return — especially at long range. Faster rotation means quicker updates on the display, which is great for tracking fast-moving targets but fewer pulses hit each target, reducing effective detection range. So you're trading detection range for update rate again.
exam_tip: Slower rotation = more pulses per target, longer range. Faster rotation = quicker updates, shorter effective range.
key_facts: Antenna rotation rate controls how often each target is illuminated
Slower rotation: more pulses hit each target, better detection at range
Faster rotation: faster screen updates but fewer pulses per target
Trade-off between detection range and update rate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; standard radar theory

syllabus_desc: State the maximum range of PSR in New Zealand
concept: The maximum operational range of PSR in New Zealand is typically around 60 nm from the radar head. That's shorter than the theoretical limit of the equipment — PSR can physically detect returns out to well over 100 nm — but line-of-sight, terrain shadowing (plenty of that in NZ), and operational accuracy requirements bring the usable figure down. Because NZ has only limited PSR coverage (mostly around the main centres), most of the en-route surveillance in controlled airspace is done by SSR instead.
exam_tip: NZ PSR max operational range: around 60 nm. Remember that terrain and line-of-sight limit it well below the equipment's theoretical range.
