key_facts: Maximum operational range of PSR in NZ is approximately 60 nm
Limited by line-of-sight, terrain shadowing and accuracy requirements
NZ PSR coverage is limited — most surveillance uses SSR
The 60 nm figure is the operational limit, not the technical limit of the equipment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; standard radar theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of SSR
concept: Secondary Surveillance Radar is a much smarter system than PSR. Instead of relying on a passive echo, the ground station transmits an interrogation signal on 1030 MHz, and every aeroplane carrying a transponder replies on 1090 MHz with a coded data message. The ground station measures the time between interrogation and reply to get the range, uses its antenna bearing for direction, and decodes the reply to get the aircraft identity and (in Mode C or S) its pressure altitude. The result: range, bearing, identity and altitude, all in one package. No more guessing which blob on the screen is which — each target on the controller's display is labelled with its callsign and level.
exam_tip: SSR: interrogate on 1030 MHz, reply on 1090 MHz. Gives range, bearing, ID and (Mode C/S) altitude. Requires the aircraft to carry a working transponder.
key_facts: Ground station transmits interrogations on 1030 MHz
Airborne transponder replies on 1090 MHz with coded data
Range is calculated from interrogation-to-reply time
Bearing comes from the directional ground antenna
Reply carries identity and (Mode C/S) altitude
Requires a serviceable transponder in the aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; standard SSR theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors affecting the operational range of SSR
concept: SSR range is limited mostly by line-of-sight, because both the interrogation and the reply are at VHF/UHF-ish frequencies that don't bend around the Earth or through terrain. So the usual rule-of-thumb applies: higher aircraft and higher ground antenna = longer range. Transmitter power (both on the ground and in the transponder), antenna gain, receiver sensitivity and the transponder's reply efficiency all play a part, but line-of-sight is the dominant factor. Terrain shadowing, garbled replies (when two aircraft reply simultaneously from similar bearings), and interference from other radars can all cut into the effective usable range.
exam_tip: SSR range is limited primarily by line-of-sight. Also affected by transmitter power, antenna gain, and garbling/interference.
key_facts: Primary limit is line-of-sight between ground antenna and aircraft
Higher aircraft altitude = longer range
Transmitter power and antenna gain affect usable range
Garbled replies (overlapping replies from nearby aircraft) reduce effective range
Terrain shadowing can block coverage even within theoretical line-of-sight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; standard SSR theory

syllabus_desc: State the maximum operational range of the SSR system in New Zealand
concept: In New Zealand, the maximum operational range of SSR is around 250 nm from the ground station — four times the PSR figure. That's why SSR does the heavy lifting for en-route surveillance in NZ controlled airspace: a handful of SSR heads can cover most of the country above about 5,000–10,000 ft, where terrain stops being an issue. Below that, coverage gets patchy in valleys and behind hills, which is one of the reasons lower-level IFR operations in NZ rely heavily on procedural separation and pilot position reports in uncontrolled areas.
exam_tip: NZ SSR max operational range: around 250 nm. That's why SSR, not PSR, does most of the surveillance work in NZ.
key_facts: Maximum operational range of SSR in NZ is approximately 250 nm
About four times the range of PSR
Provides most of the en-route surveillance coverage in NZ controlled airspace
Coverage is still limited by terrain at lower levels
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; standard SSR theory

syllabus_desc: State the advantages of SSR over PSR
concept: SSR wins over PSR in most respects. It gives you positive identification (no guessing which blob is which), it gives you altitude from Mode C/S, the replies are far stronger than passive echoes so range is much greater, and weather returns don't clutter the display because you're only seeing transponder replies, not reflections off rain. SSR is also less affected by the size and shape of the aircraft — a small GA machine is just as visible as an airliner, provided both have working transponders. The catch, of course, is that SSR only sees aeroplanes that are cooperating — if the transponder is off or broken, SSR sees nothing. PSR sees it regardless.
exam_tip: SSR advantages: positive ID, altitude reporting, longer range, no weather clutter, size-independent. Disadvantage: needs a working transponder.
key_facts: Provides positive identification of each aircraft
Provides altitude information (Mode C and Mode S)
Much longer range than PSR — around 250 nm vs 60 nm in NZ
Not affected by weather returns or target size/material
Requires aircraft to be carrying a serviceable transponder
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; standard SSR theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the following functions on a typical transponder control panel: standby (SBY)
concept: Standby (SBY) keeps the transponder powered up and warm but not replying to interrogations. Think of it as 'I'm here and ready, but not talking yet'. You select SBY on start-up and during taxi until ATC tells you otherwise (or until you're about to line up on the runway), so the unit is warm and stable by the time you need it. Modern installations are often wired to go straight to ALT on the ground at controlled aerodromes for A-SMGCS surveillance — check your local procedures.
exam_tip: SBY = powered, warmed up, not replying. Used while the transponder is warming up and before it's needed.
key_facts: Transponder is powered and warm but does not respond to interrogations
Used during taxi or on the ground where replies aren't required
Allows the unit to stabilise before transmitting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.6; NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the following functions on a typical transponder control panel: ON
concept: ON (sometimes labelled Mode A) tells the transponder to reply to interrogations with its selected 4-digit code only — no altitude. It's the minimum required for identification. In practice you'll rarely sit in plain ON for long; most modern installations and ATC procedures want altitude reporting, so ALT is the usual selection once airborne. Some older aeroplanes with unserviceable altitude encoders have to operate in ON.
exam_tip: ON = replies with code only, no altitude. Essentially Mode A operation.
key_facts: Transponder replies to interrogations with the selected 4-digit squawk code
Does not transmit altitude information
Equivalent to Mode A operation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.6; NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the following functions on a typical transponder control panel: ALT
concept: ALT (sometimes labelled Mode C) is the normal operating position. The transponder replies with the selected 4-digit code plus pressure altitude from the encoder. This is what ATC actually wants — it's what drives the altitude tag next to your target on the controller's screen. In NZ controlled airspace, operating in ALT is the default assumption any time you're flying.
exam_tip: ALT = code + altitude. This is the normal in-flight selection in NZ controlled airspace.
key_facts: Transponder replies with 4-digit code plus pressure altitude
Equivalent to Mode C operation
Normal in-flight selection
Required in most NZ controlled airspace
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.6; NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the following functions on a typical transponder control panel: test (TST)
concept: TST (test) lets you check the transponder is basically working before flight. Pressing TST runs an internal self-test of the circuitry and lights the reply monitor lamp briefly to show you it's transmitting. It's a quick serviceability check, not a full system test — it doesn't guarantee the encoder or the antenna are fine, just that the box itself powers up, self-tests, and can generate a reply.
exam_tip: TST = internal self-test, lights the reply lamp briefly. Confirms the box itself is working, not the whole system.
key_facts: Runs an internal self-test of the transponder's circuits
Briefly illuminates the reply monitor light
Does not verify the encoder or antenna independently
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.6; NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the following functions on a typical transponder control panel: IDENT
concept: IDENT is the 'it's me' button. When ATC says 'squawk ident', you press it once and the transponder sends a Special Position Identification (SPI) pulse along with the next few replies. On the controller's screen, your target gets a distinctive flash or a temporarily enlarged symbol that lasts about 20 seconds, making you unmistakable among the traffic. Only press it when ATC asks, or when you want to draw attention to yourself — pressing it unprompted is poor form and clutters the picture for the controller.
exam_tip: IDENT = sends the SPI pulse that makes your target flash on ATC's screen for about 20 seconds. Only press it when asked, or when you need attention.
key_facts: Sends a Special Position Identification (SPI) pulse with subsequent replies
Makes the target flash or highlight on the controller's display for about 20 seconds
Only use when requested by ATC, or when you need to attract ATC's attention
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.6; NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the following functions on a typical transponder control panel: code selection controls
concept: The code selection controls are how you set the 4-digit squawk code. On older units that means four concentric thumbwheels or knobs, each set to a digit 0–7 (transponder codes are octal — no 8s or 9s). Newer units have push-buttons or a keypad where you type the code and push ENTER or IDENT. Whatever the interface, the code you set is what the transponder sends back when interrogated. Double-check the numbers before pushing ENT, and never 'roll through' an emergency code on the way to your assigned one — more on that in a moment.
exam_tip: Four digits, each 0–7 (octal). Knobs on old units, push-buttons on new. Always verify the code before committing.
key_facts: Four digits, each 0–7 (octal system — no 8s or 9s)
Older units use rotary thumbwheels; newer units use push-buttons or a keypad
Always double-check the code before committing it
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.6; NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the following functions on a typical transponder control panel: reply monitor light
concept: The reply monitor light — usually a small R or REPLY lamp — flashes briefly each time the transponder replies to an interrogation. In an area with good SSR coverage it'll be blinking constantly; in thin coverage it'll be sporadic. If it's not flashing at all when you expect it to be, something's wrong — bad code, wrong mode, antenna fault, or you're out of coverage. It's your only visual feedback that the box is actually talking to anyone.
exam_tip: The reply light flashes with every reply. No flashes in coverage = investigate. It's your only feedback the transponder is transmitting.
key_facts: Flashes briefly with each transponder reply
Constant flashing in good SSR coverage is normal
Absence of flashing in coverage areas indicates a problem
The only direct cockpit indication that the transponder is transmitting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.6; NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the correct use of the IDENT button (or switch)
concept: Press IDENT only when ATC asks — 'squawk ident' — and only press it once. A single firm push is all that's needed; the SPI pulse lasts around 20 seconds on the controller's screen regardless of how long you hold it. Don't press it repeatedly, don't press it just to see the light flash, and don't press it unprompted unless you're trying to get ATC's attention (for example if your radio has gone quiet and you need to flag yourself). Pressing IDENT when no one has asked clutters the controller's screen and can confuse them about which aeroplane they were actually talking to.
exam_tip: Single push, only when ATC asks. The SPI pulse lasts ~20 seconds automatically. Don't hold it, don't spam it.
key_facts: Press only when ATC requests 'squawk ident'
A single press is sufficient — the SPI pulse runs for about 20 seconds
Do not press repeatedly or hold the button
Can be used to attract ATC attention in communication failure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.6; NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain the meanings of typical transponder terminology
concept: A few terms come up again and again. Mode A is code-only replies (identification). Mode C is code plus pressure altitude. Mode S is a more capable system that exchanges selective data with the ground (aircraft ID, altitude, and much more) and is required for most modern ADS-B operations. A squawk code is the 4-digit octal code you've set. The interrogator is the ground station asking; the transponder is your airborne responder. The reply is the coded data you send back. 'Squawk ident' means push the IDENT button; 'squawk standby' means select SBY; 'stop squawk' means you should stop transmitting transponder replies.
exam_tip: Know Mode A vs C vs S, and know what 'squawk ident', 'squawk standby' and 'stop squawk' mean in plain English.
key_facts: Mode A: code only (identification)
Mode C: code plus pressure altitude
Mode S: selective addressing, data exchange, supports ADS-B
Squawk code: 4-digit octal identifier
'Squawk ident' = press IDENT; 'Squawk standby' = select SBY; 'Stop squawk' = stop replying
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.6; NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the operational limits of Mode C readouts
concept: Mode C altitude is pressure altitude — always referenced to 1013.2 hPa, regardless of what you have set on your altimeter subscale. The ATC computer applies your QNH correction at its end to display the level the controller sees. Mode C is reported in 100 ft increments, so it's quantised — you won't see finer than that. The operational limit is a tolerance of ±200 ft between the transmitted Mode C altitude and your indicated altitude on 1013; more than that and the altitude reporting is considered unreliable and ATC will ask you to stop altitude squawking (or stop squawk altogether). Mode C also has an upper altitude limit built into the encoder — most commercial encoders go up to around 62,700 ft.
exam_tip: Mode C: pressure altitude (1013 hPa), reported in 100 ft increments, tolerance ±200 ft. ATC computer applies QNH at their end.
key_facts: Mode C transmits pressure altitude referenced to 1013.2 hPa
Reported in 100 ft increments
Tolerance is ±200 ft versus the aircraft's indicated altitude on 1013
ATC system applies the QNH correction for controller display
Typical encoder upper limit around 62,700 ft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.6; NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the transponder emergency codes
concept: Three emergency codes, and you need to know them all cold: 7500 for unlawful interference (hijack), 7600 for communication failure, and 7700 for a general emergency (anything else — engine failure, medical, fire, and so on). Easy way to remember: seven-five you're in a dive (being hijacked — bad), seven-six radio nix (radios out), seven-seven heading to heaven (any serious emergency). Setting any of these flags your target on every ATC screen that can see you — SSR is nationwide, so help is on its way within seconds of you twisting the knobs.
exam_tip: 7500 = unlawful interference. 7600 = comms failure. 7700 = general emergency. Memorise these — no excuses.
key_facts: 7500 — unlawful interference (hijack)
7600 — communication failure
7700 — general emergency
Any of these codes triggers an alert on all ATC displays
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.6; NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the operational precautions required when changing codes
concept: When you change codes, never let the thumbwheels roll through an emergency squawk on the way to the new one — if you're going from, say, 1200 to 7000, rotate each digit directly rather than counting up through 7500, 7600 or 7700. On older mechanical units this was a real issue; modern push-button units let you type the new code before committing, which avoids the problem entirely. Either way, the safest method is to select SBY first, set the new code, then return to ALT. That way the transponder can't send any intermediate code accidentally, and ATC never sees a momentary emergency squawk from you. It's a small habit that saves controllers a lot of stress.
exam_tip: Cardinal rule: never roll through an emergency code. Best practice: SBY → set new code → ALT.
key_facts: Do not allow the transponder to transmit intermediate emergency codes while changing
On rotary units, rotate each digit directly rather than counting through 7500/7600/7700
Best practice: select SBY, set the new code, return to ALT
Push-button units largely eliminate the rolling-through problem
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 1.6; NZ CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of airborne weather radar
concept: Airborne weather radar is basically a small, forward-looking PSR in the nose of your aeroplane. It transmits a narrow beam of radio energy from a steerable dish in the radome, and listens for the reflections coming back from water droplets in the air ahead. The bigger the droplets and the more of them there are, the stronger the return — which is why weather radar sees rain, hail and wet snow really well, but barely sees dry snow, cloud, turbulence or clear air at all. The display colour-codes returns by intensity (typically green/yellow/red/magenta from weakest to strongest), so you can spot heavy precipitation cells long before you see them visually. It's a painting of moisture, not a painting of weather per se — and knowing the difference is the whole skill of using it well.
exam_tip: Weather radar sees water droplets. It paints rain, not turbulence. Bigger drops, more drops = stronger return. Colour = intensity.
key_facts: Forward-looking pulsed radar in the nose radome
Detects returns from water droplets in the air ahead
Bigger and more numerous droplets give stronger returns
Display colour-codes intensity — typically green → yellow → red → magenta
Does not directly detect turbulence, cloud, dry snow or clear air
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; standard airborne weather radar theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of: the tilt control
concept: The tilt control adjusts the vertical angle of the antenna. Tilt it up and you're looking higher above the horizon; tilt it down and you're looking at the terrain and weather closer to your level or below. The right tilt depends on your altitude and range setting: cruising at FL180 with the range at 80 nm, you typically tilt slightly down to paint returns ahead at your level without catching ground returns close in. Tilt wrong and you either miss weather (beam too high) or flood the screen with ground clutter (beam too low). Experienced operators constantly adjust tilt as they fly — it's not a set-and-forget control.
exam_tip: Tilt = vertical angle of the antenna. Adjust constantly based on altitude and range. Too high = miss weather; too low = ground clutter.
key_facts: Adjusts the vertical angle of the antenna beam
Must be adjusted with altitude and range
Too much up tilt: weather at your level is missed
Too much down tilt: ground returns flood the display
Requires constant monitoring and adjustment in flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; standard airborne weather radar theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of: the range control
concept: The range control sets how far ahead the radar is looking, typically in steps from about 10 nm out to 300 nm or more. Shorter ranges give more detail on nearby weather; longer ranges let you plan deviations well in advance. Good technique is to alternate: use a long range periodically to scan for distant cells, then drop back to a shorter range to study the ones that matter right now. Remember that at longer ranges the beam is wider and pointed at a higher absolute altitude at any given tilt setting, so tilt generally needs adjusting whenever you change range.
exam_tip: Range control = how far ahead you're looking. Alternate long and short ranges. Always re-check tilt when you change range.
key_facts: Selects the maximum range displayed
Short range: more detail on nearby weather
Long range: early warning of distant cells
Tilt typically needs to be re-checked whenever range is changed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; standard airborne weather radar theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of: the ANT STAB switch
concept: The ANT STAB (antenna stabilisation) switch enables or disables the gyro-stabilisation of the antenna. With stabilisation on, the antenna platform is slaved to the aircraft's attitude gyros so it stays pointed at the correct beam angle regardless of what the aeroplane is doing in pitch and roll — in turns, the antenna keeps looking straight ahead relative to the horizon, not dragged around with the bank. Without stabilisation, in a 30-degree bank the beam tilts 30 degrees across the horizon and paints one side of the display as 'up into the sky' and the other as 'down at the ground'. You always want stabilisation on in normal operation.
exam_tip: ANT STAB ON = antenna stays level in turns. Without it, the beam tilts with the aeroplane and the picture becomes useless during manoeuvring.
key_facts: Stabilises the antenna platform against aircraft pitch and roll
Keeps the beam pointing at the correct angle regardless of attitude
Without stabilisation, the picture distorts badly in turns
Normally left on at all times in flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; standard airborne weather radar theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of: the GAIN control
concept: The GAIN control adjusts the receiver's sensitivity — essentially how much it amplifies weak returns. In AUTO (or CAL/CALIBRATED), gain is fixed at a calibrated level so that the colour coding reflects true precipitation intensity — that's the mode you use for normal operation and for making decisions about where to fly. Manual gain lets you turn sensitivity up to enhance weak returns (useful at long range) or down to reduce clutter (useful in rain). But if you're in manual gain, the colour-intensity relationship is no longer accurate — a red return doesn't necessarily mean a 'red' (heavy) cell, it just means 'loud compared to current gain setting'. Decision-making should always be done in AUTO/CAL gain.
exam_tip: Use AUTO/CAL gain for decision-making — colours mean what they're supposed to mean. Manual gain is only for investigating, never for deciding.
key_facts: GAIN adjusts receiver sensitivity
AUTO/CAL mode maintains calibrated colour-to-intensity relationship
Manual gain breaks the calibration — colours no longer reflect true intensity
Use AUTO/CAL for weather avoidance decisions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; standard airborne weather radar theory

syllabus_desc: Interpret the indications from a weather radar, in its various modes
concept: In weather mode, the standard interpretation is: black/no return = no significant precipitation; green = light precipitation; yellow = moderate; red = heavy; magenta = very heavy or possibly turbulent (on sets with a turbulence/TURB mode, magenta specifically indicates detected turbulence within precipitation). Sharp, concave edges, bright cores, scalloped or fingered returns, and rapid colour gradients from green straight to red are all signs of strong convective activity with likely severe turbulence — avoid those cells by at least 20 nm, and more upwind or downwind as needed. Smooth, widespread green with no strong gradients is usually stratiform rain, much less of a concern for the airframe. In map mode (where fitted), the same antenna is used to paint ground features for navigation, but the display interpretation is completely different.
exam_tip: Colours: green/yellow/red/magenta = light/moderate/heavy/severe. Sharp gradients, scalloping, bright cores = convective, avoid by ≥20 nm.
key_facts: Black: no significant precipitation
Green: light precipitation
Yellow: moderate precipitation
Red: heavy precipitation
Magenta: very heavy or turbulent
Sharp gradients and scalloped edges indicate convective activity — avoid by at least 20 nm
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; standard airborne weather radar theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the weather radar return strengths of various types of precipitation
concept: Not all precipitation looks the same to weather radar. The radar sees large water droplets far better than small ones, and wet surfaces far better than dry ones. Heavy rain (big drops) gives the strongest returns — easy to spot, painted red or magenta. Wet hail is even stronger — ice coated in water reflects very aggressively, which is why the hottest cores of convective cells often show magenta. Dry hail is weaker but still visible. Wet snow (melting snow aloft) shows up reasonably well, especially at the freezing level. Dry snow and ice crystals give very weak returns because the small ice particles are poor reflectors — you can fly into serious snow and see almost nothing on the screen. Drizzle and cloud droplets are basically invisible. The golden rule: no return does not mean no weather. Always cross-reference with temperature, convective potential, PIREPs and your eyes.
exam_tip: Strongest returns: wet hail → heavy rain → wet snow. Weakest: dry snow, drizzle, cloud droplets. No paint ≠ no weather.
key_facts: Wet hail: strongest returns of all
Heavy rain: strong returns
Wet (melting) snow: moderate-to-strong returns
Dry hail: weaker but still visible
Dry snow and ice crystals: very weak returns
Drizzle, cloud droplets: essentially invisible to weather radar
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; standard airborne weather radar theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the purpose of approach lighting systems and distinguish between the three types of system commonly used in New Zealand
concept: Approach lighting systems do one job: help you transition from the instrument world into the visual world at the end of an instrument approach. On a dark night or in marginal visibility, they give you a long illuminated lead-in to the runway threshold — crucial for lining up, judging height, and identifying which runway you're actually looking at. In New Zealand you'll typically see three types: a simple approach lighting system (short, basic — a line of white lights leading to the threshold), a precision approach category I system (longer, with crossbar, used at ILS runways), and, at a few major aerodromes, more elaborate Cat II/III style systems with additional sidebar and centreline lighting. The AIP NZ AD chart for each aerodrome tells you exactly what's installed and what length it is.
exam_tip: Three NZ approach lighting types: simple, precision Cat I, more elaborate Cat II/III at major centres. Purpose: transition from IMC to visual reference.
key_facts: Purpose: assist the transition from instrument to visual flight at decision altitude/MDA
Simple approach lighting system: short line of lights leading to threshold
Precision Cat I system: longer with crossbar, used at ILS runways
Cat II/III systems at major aerodromes with additional sidebar and centreline lighting
AIP NZ AD chart specifies what is installed at each aerodrome
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe the purpose of circling guidance lighting and runway lead-in lighting
concept: Circling guidance lights and runway lead-in lights do exactly what they sound like. Circling guidance lighting is used at aerodromes where the circling approach track doesn't line up with the normal approach lighting — it provides visual cues along the circling track so you can fly it safely at night. Lead-in lighting is a string of flashing lights leading from some notable feature (like a coastline or a gap in terrain) to the approach end of the runway, used where the approach must follow a non-straight track to avoid obstacles or terrain. You'll find lead-in lights at a few terrain-constrained NZ aerodromes where a straight-in isn't practical.
exam_tip: Circling guidance = lights along the circling track. Lead-in = flashing lights guiding you from a landmark to the runway when the approach is non-straight.
key_facts: Circling guidance lighting provides visual cues along the circling approach track
Used where the circling track doesn't align with normal approach lighting
Lead-in lighting is a string of flashing lights from a feature to the runway threshold
Used where terrain or obstacles require a non-straight approach
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Interpret the approach slope indications given by: T-VASIS
concept: T-VASIS (the Australian/NZ T-shaped Visual Approach Slope Indicator System) uses a pattern of white lights either side of the runway that forms a 'T' shape. When you're on the correct slope, you see just the horizontal bar of the T — no vertical stem. If you're too high, fly-down bars appear above the horizontal bar (more bars = further above slope). If you're too low, fly-up bars appear below it (more bars = further below slope). Dangerously low: the horizontal bar turns red. The mnemonic is straightforward: 'more bars up, fly up; more bars down, fly down'. T-VASIS is being phased out in favour of PAPI at many aerodromes, but you'll still meet it at some regional sites.
exam_tip: T-VASIS: on slope = just the horizontal bar. Bars above = too high (fly down). Bars below = too low (fly up). Red bar = dangerously low.
key_facts: T-shaped array of white lights either side of the runway
On slope: only the horizontal bar visible
Fly-down bars above the bar: you're high (more bars = higher)
Fly-up bars below the bar: you're low (more bars = lower)
Horizontal bar turns red when dangerously low
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Interpret the approach slope indications given by: RAE red-white VASIS
concept: RAE (Royal Aircraft Establishment) red-white VASIS uses two rows of lights, near and far, each showing either red or white depending on your position relative to the slope. On slope: the near bar shows white and the far bar shows red — 'white over red, you're dead... no, you're fine' (sorry, bad pilot humour). Too high: both bars white. Too low: both bars red. Slightly high: white over white; slightly low: red over red. Dangerously low: both red, very red. Not as intuitive as PAPI but it's still around at a few locations.
exam_tip: Red-white VASIS: 'white over red, on the centred'. Both white = high. Both red = low.
key_facts: Two rows of bars — near and far — each red or white
On slope: near bar white, far bar red
Both white: too high
Both red: too low
Standard mnemonic: 'white over red, on the slope'
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Interpret the approach slope indications given by: precision approach path indicator (PAPI) and abbreviated PAPI
concept: PAPI (Precision Approach Path Indicator) is a row of four lights to one side of the runway (usually the left), each of which shows either white (if you're above its slope angle) or red (if you're below). On slope you see two white and two red — 'two white, two red, on the glide'. Slightly high: three white, one red. High: all four white. Slightly low: three red, one white. Low: all four red. Clean, fast to interpret, and standard worldwide. Abbreviated PAPI (APAPI) uses only two lights instead of four — on slope is one red, one white — and is installed where space or cost precludes full PAPI, typically at smaller aerodromes.
exam_tip: PAPI: two white + two red = on slope. All white = high. All red = low. APAPI: two lights, one red + one white = on slope.
key_facts: PAPI: four lights in a row — white (above slope) or red (below slope)
On slope: two white on one side, two red on the other (two and two)
All four white: too high
All four red: too low
APAPI (abbreviated): two lights — one red, one white on slope
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Explain the standard PAPI angle setting, and the setting of PAPI threshold crossing height (TCH)
concept: The standard PAPI angle is 3 degrees — same as a standard ILS glideslope — though it can be adjusted upward for specific obstacle clearance or noise abatement reasons at some sites. The threshold crossing height (TCH) is set so that an aeroplane on the PAPI slope crosses the runway threshold at a height that gives adequate wheel clearance above the threshold for the biggest aeroplanes normally using the runway — typically in the range 40–60 ft for regional runways and higher for international runways serving wide-body aircraft. At smaller GA aerodromes, the TCH may be lower to suit light aircraft. Check the AIP NZ chart for non-standard values before flying an unfamiliar PAPI.
exam_tip: Standard PAPI angle: 3 degrees. TCH is set to give safe wheel clearance for the design aircraft — check the chart for non-standard values.
key_facts: Standard PAPI slope angle is 3 degrees
May be set higher at specific aerodromes for obstacle or noise reasons
Threshold crossing height (TCH) gives adequate wheel clearance at the threshold
TCH varies by aerodrome design aircraft — check the AIP NZ chart
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: State the possible atmospheric effects on approach slope indication
concept: Atmospheric effects can distort the apparent slope indication. Refraction through layers of differing temperature (very common in NZ's temperature-inverted conditions over cool lakes and morning valleys) can bend the light rays slightly, making the slope indication appear different from reality — you might see an 'on-slope' indication while you're actually a fraction high or low. Rain and fog scatter and absorb light, reducing contrast and making the colour transition less sharp (the red-white boundary becomes fuzzier, and the point at which the colour changes is less precise). Haze reduces brightness and contrast generally. None of these effects are huge, but on a marginal approach they matter — always cross-check visual slope guidance against your instruments (vertical speed, altitude vs distance to go) when conditions aren't ideal.
exam_tip: Atmospheric effects: refraction through temperature layers can distort slope; rain, fog, haze reduce contrast and sharpness of the transition. Always cross-check against the panel.
key_facts: Refraction through temperature layers can bend light and shift apparent slope
Rain and fog reduce contrast and blur the red/white transition
Haze reduces brightness and contrast overall
Cross-check visual slope against vertical speed and distance-to-go in marginal conditions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe typical layout and presentation of the following lighting: normal runway
concept: A standard runway lighting layout at a lit aerodrome includes: runway edge lights (white, in a line down each side of the runway, yellow for the last 600 m or one-third of the runway, whichever is less, to indicate the approaching end); threshold lights (a green line across the threshold, visible only from the approach direction); runway end lights (a red line across the far end, visible from the runway); and centreline lights on longer, precision-approach runways (white fading to alternating red/white and then all red toward the end). Spacing is standardised — edge lights typically every 60 m on precision runways. You're not expected to memorise every dimension, but know the colours and what each row means.
exam_tip: Edge = white (yellow last 600 m/one-third), threshold = green (from approach), runway end = red (from runway), centreline = white fading to red/white then red.
key_facts: Runway edge lights: white, yellow for the last 600 m or one-third
Threshold lights: green, across the threshold (visible from approach direction)
Runway end lights: red, across the far end (visible from the runway)
Centreline lights: white, transitioning to alternating red/white and then red
Edge light spacing typically 60 m on precision runways
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe typical layout and presentation of the following lighting: displaced threshold
concept: A displaced threshold is where the landing threshold is moved down the runway from the physical end — typically because of obstacle clearance on the approach. The lighting reflects this: the displaced threshold itself has the green threshold line, and the runway surface before it (usable for takeoff and rollout but not for landing) is marked by white edge lights that continue back to the physical runway end, with additional green wing-bar lights at the displaced threshold to make it obvious from the air. Landing before the displaced threshold is a big deal — the lighting is designed to make sure you don't.
exam_tip: Displaced threshold = landing point moved down the runway. Green threshold line + green wing bars at the displaced threshold. Edge lights continue to the physical end.
key_facts: Landing threshold is displaced from the physical runway end for obstacle clearance
Green threshold line marks the displaced threshold
Green wing-bar lights are added at the displaced threshold for visibility
Edge lights continue along the pre-threshold area to the physical runway end
Area before the displaced threshold may be used for takeoff and rollout, not landing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe typical layout and presentation of the following lighting: runway touchdown zone
concept: Runway touchdown zone (TDZ) lighting, where installed (on precision approach runways at larger aerodromes), is an array of white light bars set into the runway surface on either side of the centreline, extending from the threshold to about 900 m down the runway (or half the runway length, whichever is less). It gives an immediate visual cue for aiming and flare height in the first section of the runway — invaluable in low visibility. Smaller NZ aerodromes typically don't have TDZ lighting.
exam_tip: TDZ lighting = in-pavement white bars from threshold to ~900 m down the runway, either side of centreline. Precision runways at major aerodromes only.
key_facts: In-pavement white light bars either side of the runway centreline
Extends from the threshold to about 900 m (or half runway length) down the runway
Aids touchdown aiming and flare height judgement in low visibility
Installed at precision approach runways at larger aerodromes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe typical layout and presentation of the following lighting: runway end indicator lighting
concept: Runway end indicator lighting (REIL) is a pair of synchronised flashing white strobe lights, one on each side of the runway threshold, angled toward the approach. They flash to draw your attention to the runway end in low visibility, against a cluttered background, or at aerodromes where the threshold is hard to distinguish from surrounding terrain and lights. They're especially useful at smaller aerodromes that don't have a full approach lighting system. When you're trying to pick out the right runway among city lights, REILs are the first thing that catches your eye.
exam_tip: REIL = two flashing white strobes at the threshold, drawing your eye to the runway. Not the same as approach lighting.
key_facts: Pair of synchronised flashing white strobe lights at the threshold
One on each side, aimed at the approach direction
Draws attention to the threshold in low visibility or cluttered backgrounds
Often installed at aerodromes without a full approach lighting system
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe typical layout and presentation of the following lighting: taxiway
concept: Taxiway lighting is blue (edge lights) and green (centreline lights, where fitted). Blue edge lights mark the sides of every taxiway in use at night. Green centreline lights are used at larger aerodromes to guide aircraft along the taxi route, especially through complicated intersections or in low visibility. At junctions, stop bars (a row of red lights across the taxiway) are used at busy aerodromes to prevent runway incursions — you don't cross a red stop bar, ever, without explicit clearance. At smaller NZ aerodromes you might just have blue edge lights and reflective edge markers.
exam_tip: Taxiway edge = blue. Taxiway centreline = green. Stop bar = red (never cross without clearance).
key_facts: Taxiway edge lights are blue
Taxiway centreline lights (where installed) are green
Stop bars are red — never cross without explicit clearance
Larger aerodromes use centreline and stop bars; smaller ones rely on edge lights and markers
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe typical layout and presentation of the following lighting: wind direction indicator lighting
concept: Illuminated wind direction indicators (the classic wind sock in its cone or framework) are lit either internally or by floodlights at night-rated aerodromes, so you can still read the wind at any time. Some sites also have illuminated wind tees or tetrahedrons. You'll find a lit wind sock somewhere near the threshold area or the control point at any aerodrome that operates at night.
exam_tip: Lit wind socks are standard at night-rated aerodromes. Look for the sock near the threshold or control point.
key_facts: Wind direction indicators are illuminated at night-rated aerodromes
May be internally lit or floodlit
Usually located near the threshold area or aerodrome control point
Essential for confirming wind before landing at night
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe typical layout and presentation of the following lighting: aerodrome beacons
concept: An aerodrome beacon is a rotating or flashing light visible from a great distance, used to identify the aerodrome from the air at night. Civil land aerodrome beacons flash white and green alternately (white is the aerodrome identifier, green means 'this is a lit civil aerodrome'). Military aerodromes show two white flashes plus a green flash. At smaller NZ aerodromes there may be no beacon at all; at the main centres, you'll see them rotating slowly on a mast near the control tower or terminal. The beacon draws your eye long before any runway lights become visible.
exam_tip: Civil land aerodrome beacon = white and green alternating. Military = two white + one green.
key_facts: Used to identify the aerodrome from a distance at night
Civil land aerodrome: alternating white and green flashes
Military aerodrome: two white flashes followed by a green flash
Usually mounted on a mast near the control tower or terminal
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe typical layout and presentation of the following lighting: obstruction lighting
concept: Obstruction lighting marks objects that could hit an aeroplane — buildings, masts, cranes, terrain features, power lines, wind turbines. Low-intensity obstructions get steady red lights (the familiar red top-light on buildings); higher or more critical obstructions get flashing red or flashing white strobes visible from further away. The exact colour and flash rate depend on the height of the obstruction and whether the lighting is visible day and night or night-only. Wind turbines, which are tall and often in clusters, typically have synchronised red strobes on top so pilots can identify them clearly.
exam_tip: Obstruction lighting: steady red for low, flashing red or white strobes for high/critical. Colour and flash depend on height and day/night usage.
key_facts: Marks objects that pose a hazard to aircraft — buildings, masts, cranes, turbines
Low obstructions: steady red light
Higher obstructions: flashing red or flashing white strobes
Specification depends on height and whether the marker is day- or night-only
Wind turbines are often fitted with synchronised red strobes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe typical layout and presentation of the following lighting: aeronautical/marine beacons
concept: Aeronautical beacons include aerodrome beacons (already covered) and en-route lights along airways in some parts of the world (largely obsolete now but still in use in a few places). Marine beacons are the navigation lights of the maritime world — lighthouses, lit buoys, harbour entrance lights — and while you're not navigating by them, they can be surprisingly useful landmarks at night for pilots, especially around harbours like Auckland or Wellington where the marine lighting defines a clear pattern that helps you orient yourself before you see runway lights. Marine lights generally have very different characters (colour, flash pattern) from aviation lights, so you can tell them apart.
exam_tip: Aeronautical beacons are aviation lights (aerodrome, en-route). Marine beacons are lighthouses and lit buoys. Different characteristics — don't mistake one for the other.
key_facts: Aeronautical beacons include aerodrome beacons and (rarely) en-route beacons
Marine beacons are lighthouses, lit buoys, harbour entrance lights
Marine lighting has distinct colours and flash patterns from aviation lighting
Marine lights can be useful orientation cues around coastal NZ aerodromes at night
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe pilot activated lighting (PAL)
concept: Pilot Activated Lighting (PAL) is what it sounds like — runway lighting that you turn on from the cockpit via the radio. At unattended night-rated aerodromes (most of NZ outside the main centres), nobody is sitting in a tower waiting to flick the lights on for you. Instead, you key your microphone on the aerodrome's PAL frequency a set number of times within a set period, and the lighting comes on automatically for a fixed duration (typically 15 or 30 minutes). Before the duration expires, you re-key to refresh the timer. It's cheap, reliable, and means aerodromes can offer night operations without full-time staffing. The AIP NZ lists PAL-equipped aerodromes and the frequency to use.
exam_tip: PAL = pilot activates the runway lights by keying the radio. Used at unattended night-rated aerodromes. Check AIP NZ for the frequency.
key_facts: Runway lighting activated by the pilot via VHF radio keys
Used at unattended night-rated aerodromes
Lighting comes on for a fixed duration (commonly 15 or 30 minutes)
Frequency is published in AIP NZ AD entry for the aerodrome
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe the standard system of keying PAL to: activate lighting
concept: The standard NZ system for activating PAL is to key the microphone 3 times within 5 seconds on the published frequency. That brings the lighting on at low intensity. The exact method and number of keys can vary between aerodromes — always check the AIP NZ AD entry — but the 3-in-5 low, 5-in-5 medium, 7-in-5 high pattern is the most common keying system. Once activated, the lights stay on for the set duration (typically 15–30 minutes).
exam_tip: Standard NZ PAL activation: 3 keys in 5 seconds on the published frequency = lights on (low intensity).
key_facts: Standard activation: 3 microphone keys within 5 seconds
Lights come on at low intensity
Must be done on the published PAL frequency for that aerodrome
Always check the AIP NZ AD entry for local variations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe the standard system of keying PAL to: adjust the brilliance
concept: To adjust the brilliance you key the microphone more times within the 5-second window. The standard pattern is 3 keys = low, 5 keys = medium, 7 keys = high. Select high if you're approaching through rain, haze or city light clutter and need the runway to stand out; select low on a clear dark night when full brightness would be dazzling on short final. You can change brilliance at any point during the timer — just re-key with the new number of pulses.
exam_tip: 3 keys = low, 5 keys = medium, 7 keys = high. All within a 5-second window.
key_facts: 3 keys within 5 seconds: low intensity
5 keys within 5 seconds: medium intensity
7 keys within 5 seconds: high intensity
Brilliance can be changed at any point during the timer duration
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe the standard system of keying PAL to: reactivate lighting
concept: To reactivate — in other words, to reset the timer before the lighting automatically turns off — you simply re-key the same sequence. Re-keying starts the timer over from the beginning (15 or 30 minutes depending on the installation). Good practice is to reactivate well before the timer runs out, especially during the critical phase of the approach — nothing ruins a night approach quite like the runway lights switching off on short final because you forgot to refresh. Don't leave it to the last minute.
exam_tip: Re-key the same sequence to reset the timer. Do it well before the lights are due to switch off — don't leave it to the last minute on final.
key_facts: Re-keying resets the PAL timer to full duration
Use the same keying sequence as for initial activation
Do it before the timer expires — ideally well before short final
Never rely on timing down to the last second
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Describe the means available for remote control of lighting other than PAL
concept: Other remote control options for aerodrome lighting include telephone activation (less common now), dedicated UHF or VHF radio links between AFIS/RFFS/ground operations and the lighting panel, and at some aerodromes, controls that allow the aerodrome operator to switch lighting on and off from off-airport locations. At a few larger unattended aerodromes the lighting panel is in the terminal or operations building and can be activated by authorised personnel. PAL remains the dominant system for routine pilot use in NZ, but at attended aerodromes ATC will simply switch the lights on for you when you request them on the tower frequency.
exam_tip: Besides PAL: telephone activation, dedicated radio links, manual control at attended aerodromes. PAL is the main system you'll use as a pilot.
key_facts: Telephone activation (limited use now)
Dedicated radio links for AFIS or operations staff
Manual switching from terminal or ops building at some aerodromes
At attended aerodromes, ATC or AFIS simply switches the lights on request
PAL is the dominant pilot-operated system in NZ
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ AD 1.1 and aerodrome charts; CAR Part 139

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic operating principles of a VOR ground station, including the: reference phase signal
concept: A VOR ground station transmits two signals that a receiver compares to work out your bearing from the station. The first is the reference phase signal — an omnidirectional signal that goes out equally in all directions. Its phase is fixed and the same no matter where you are relative to the station. Think of it as the clock tick: every aeroplane anywhere around the station hears the reference phase pulse at exactly the same instant. That gives your receiver a timing reference against which to compare the variable phase signal.
exam_tip: Reference phase = omnidirectional, same phase everywhere. The timing reference.
key_facts: Transmitted omnidirectionally from the VOR ground station
Phase is identical in all directions around the station
Acts as the timing reference for the receiver
Compared against the variable phase signal to derive bearing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic operating principles of a VOR ground station, including the: variable phase signal
concept: The variable phase signal is transmitted from a directional antenna (either a rotating one in conventional CVOR or an electronically scanned array in Doppler VOR) whose phase varies with direction. At magnetic north from the station, the variable phase is in phase with the reference. As you move clockwise around the station, the phase difference grows by 1 degree for every degree of bearing. At due east (090) the phase lags the reference by 90 degrees. At due south, 180 degrees. At due west, 270. So the phase difference IS the bearing from the station.
exam_tip: Variable phase: changes 1 degree per degree of bearing around the station, zero phase difference at magnetic north.
key_facts: Transmitted from a directional antenna, phase depends on direction
At magnetic north: in phase with the reference (zero phase difference)
Phase difference increases 1 degree per 1 degree of bearing clockwise
Phase difference equals the magnetic radial from the station
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic operating principles of a VOR ground station, including the: measurement of phase difference
concept: Your VOR receiver measures the phase difference between the two received signals — reference and variable — and converts it directly into a bearing from the station. That bearing is the radial you're on. The result is presented on your VOR indicator (fixed card, rotatable card, HSI or RMI needle). It's genuinely a magic bit of engineering: two signals, one phase comparison, and you get a continuous magnetic bearing from any point in the coverage area — no back-calculation, no triangulation, nothing.
exam_tip: Phase difference between reference and variable signals = radial from the station. That's the whole principle.
key_facts: Receiver measures the phase difference between reference and variable signals
The phase difference equals the magnetic bearing from the station
Converted into a radial and displayed on the VOR indicator
Gives continuous bearing from any point within coverage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Describe what is meant by a VOR radial
concept: A VOR radial is a specific magnetic bearing from the station — a line drawn outward from the VOR on a particular magnetic course. So 'the 270 radial' is the line extending due magnetic west from the station. There are 360 radials around a VOR (one per degree), and your aeroplane is always on exactly one of them at any given moment. A radial is FROM the station, not TO it, so if you're flying toward the VOR on the 090 radial, your track is 270 magnetic (the reciprocal).
exam_tip: Radial = magnetic bearing FROM the station. 360 possible radials. Flying inbound on the 090 radial = tracking 270.
key_facts: A radial is the magnetic bearing FROM the station
360 radials around each VOR — one per degree
An aircraft is always on exactly one radial
Inbound on a radial means tracking the reciprocal
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: List the publications and charts that show VOR callsigns and frequencies
concept: VOR callsigns and frequencies are published in AIP NZ Volume 4 (ENR 4.1 — Radio Navigation Aids), on New Zealand visual and instrument charts (VNCs, enroute charts, SIDs, STARs, approach plates), and in the aerodrome chart for each relevant aerodrome. The callsign is usually a 3-letter Morse identifier broadcast continuously on the voice channel of the VOR — for example, HN for Hamilton, NP for New Plymouth. Always cross-check both the chart and the actual Morse identifier before using any VOR for navigation.
exam_tip: VOR frequencies and callsigns: AIP NZ ENR 4.1, VNCs, enroute charts, SIDs/STARs, approach plates. Always cross-check both chart and Morse.
key_facts: Published in AIP NZ ENR 4.1 (Radio Navigation Aids)
Shown on Visual Navigation Charts (VNCs) and enroute charts
Shown on SIDs, STARs and approach plates
Callsign is typically a 3-letter Morse identifier
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of station identification before using the VOR
concept: Never, ever use a VOR without first positively identifying the Morse callsign. VORs go unserviceable, get switched off for maintenance, or can transmit a rogue signal if they've failed in a sneaky way — and the flag system on the indicator doesn't always catch a faulty station. The Morse ident is your confirmation that (a) you've tuned the right station, (b) it's actually transmitting, and (c) it hasn't been switched off or tagged unserviceable. If you don't hear the ident clearly, you don't use the VOR, full stop. During maintenance, the ident is usually removed from the transmission — so no ident means no ident, no nav.
exam_tip: No ident, no nav. Never use a VOR without positively hearing and identifying the Morse callsign.
key_facts: Always positively identify the Morse callsign before using a VOR
Confirms correct frequency, station identity and serviceability
The ident is removed during maintenance — no ident means the station is not usable
Flag systems are not a substitute for ident verification
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the presentation of the VOR CDI on a: fixed card
concept: On a fixed-card VOR indicator, the compass rose (or course card) doesn't rotate — it's painted on the instrument face. You set the desired course with the OBS knob, which moves a pointer or arrow to that course, and the CDI needle shows how far left or right of the set course you are. The compass card is static, so you have to mentally relate the course you've set to your aeroplane's heading. It's the simplest and oldest presentation — functional, but requires more mental work than a rotatable card or HSI.
exam_tip: Fixed card: card is painted on, doesn't rotate. You set the OBS to the desired course and the CDI shows displacement left/right of that course.
key_facts: Compass card is fixed and does not rotate
OBS knob sets the desired course
CDI needle shows displacement from the selected course
Requires mental correlation with aircraft heading
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the presentation of the VOR CDI on a: rotatable card
concept: On a rotatable-card VOR indicator (sometimes called a course deviation indicator with a rotating OBS card), the compass card is rotated by the OBS knob. You turn the knob until the course you want is at the top of the instrument, and the CDI needle shows deviation left or right of that course. This is easier than the fixed-card version because the course you've set is always presented at the top, in the natural 12-o'clock 'up is forward' orientation. Still doesn't automatically align with the aircraft heading, though — you have to keep your heading in mind.
exam_tip: Rotatable card: you turn the card so your course sits at the top. CDI shows deviation. Easier than fixed card.
key_facts: Compass card rotates with the OBS knob
Selected course is presented at the top of the instrument
CDI needle shows deviation from the course
Easier to interpret than fixed-card presentation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the presentation of the VOR CDI on a: HSI indicator
concept: An HSI (Horizontal Situation Indicator) combines the VOR CDI with the heading indicator. The compass card rotates with the aircraft heading — your current heading is always at the top — and a course arrow overlaid on the card points in the direction of the selected course. The CDI needle sits on the course arrow and shows deviation. The beauty is that everything is shown in its correct relationship: aircraft heading, selected course, and deviation, all on one instrument. No mental gymnastics needed. That's why the HSI is the standard presentation for any modern IFR aeroplane.
exam_tip: HSI = DI + VOR CDI on one face. Heading at top, course arrow overlaid, CDI on the arrow. Easiest to interpret — no mental work.
key_facts: Combines heading indicator and VOR CDI in one instrument
Compass card rotates with aircraft heading
Course arrow shows the selected course against the heading card
CDI needle sits on the course arrow showing deviation
Standard presentation on modern IFR aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a typical VOR indicator, including: the omni-bearing selector (OBS)
concept: The Omni-Bearing Selector (OBS) is the knob that lets you choose which radial (or course) you want to fly. Rotating the OBS selects a specific magnetic course and the rest of the indicator responds accordingly: the CDI shows you how far off that course you are, and the TO/FROM indicator tells you whether flying that course would take you toward the station or away from it. You use the OBS to set the outbound course you want to fly, or the inbound reciprocal when tracking to the station, or the radial you want to identify your position on.
exam_tip: OBS = the knob that selects the course you want to fly. Drives the CDI and TO/FROM indication.
key_facts: OBS selects the desired magnetic course or radial
Drives the CDI and TO/FROM indication
Used to set inbound courses to a station or outbound radials
Also used to identify which radial you're currently on
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a typical VOR indicator, including: the course deviation indicator (CDI)
concept: The Course Deviation Indicator (CDI) is the vertical needle that swings left or right to show how far off the selected course you are. Full-scale deflection is 10 degrees either side of the course on a standard VOR indicator (or 5 degrees on some HSI installations), and the needle is linear — so half-scale deflection means you're 5 degrees off (or 2.5 on the HSI). If the CDI is to the left of centre, the course is to your left — you 'fly toward the needle' to intercept it. If it's to the right, fly right.
exam_tip: CDI: full-scale = 10 degrees off on a standard VOR (5 on many HSIs). Fly toward the needle.
key_facts: Shows lateral deviation from the selected course
Full-scale deflection = 10 degrees (standard VOR) or 5 degrees (HSI)
Linear — halfway = half the full-scale displacement
Fly toward the needle to intercept the course
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a typical VOR indicator, including: the TO/FROM indicator
concept: The TO/FROM indicator tells you whether the course you've selected with the OBS would take you toward the station (TO) or away from it (FROM), if you were to fly that heading. It flips as you cross the station on a radial (or cross the perpendicular through the station), which is one of the ways you identify station passage. 'TO' doesn't mean you're heading toward the station right now — it means if you fly the selected course, you'll end up closer to the station. You need to understand this distinction cold, because misreading TO/FROM is a classic trap in the exam.
exam_tip: TO/FROM = 'would flying this course take you toward or away from the station?' — not 'am I heading toward it now?' Flips at station passage.
key_facts: Indicates whether the selected course would take you TO or FROM the station
Independent of your current heading — based on the OBS setting
Flips as you cross the station (or the perpendicular through it)
Used to confirm station passage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Describe how the VOR receiver can be used to: establish orientation of that aircraft to and from a VOR station
concept: To orient yourself relative to a VOR, tune the station, confirm the ident, and then turn the OBS until the CDI centres with a FROM indication. The number in the OBS window is the radial you're on — that is, your magnetic bearing FROM the station. To work out the bearing TO the station, either set the OBS to the reciprocal (CDI will centre with a TO indication) or just use the RMI needle pointing directly at the station if you have one. Either way, you now know exactly where you are relative to the VOR, and you can plot your position if you have a second VOR or a DME.
exam_tip: Orientation: tune, ident, OBS until CDI centres with FROM — that's your radial (bearing from station). Reciprocal = bearing to station.
key_facts: Tune and ident the VOR first
Rotate OBS until CDI centres with a FROM indication
The OBS reading is the radial you're on (bearing from the station)
Reciprocal gives bearing TO the station
RMI needle points directly at the station if fitted
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Describe how the VOR receiver can be used to: maintain a required track to a VOR station
concept: To track inbound to a VOR, set the OBS to the desired inbound course (the reciprocal of the radial you want to fly down). Turn the aeroplane to that course as a starting heading, then fly heading corrections to keep the CDI centred. The TO/FROM indicator should read TO the whole way in. As you get closer to the station, the CDI becomes more sensitive (because the radials converge), so small heading changes are needed. Expect station passage to be marked by a rapid TO/FROM flip and some needle activity.
exam_tip: Track to the station: set OBS to inbound course, fly that course, keep CDI centred. TO indication throughout. Sensitivity increases near the station.
key_facts: Set OBS to the desired inbound course
Fly that course as a heading; adjust to keep CDI centred
TO/FROM should read TO throughout
CDI sensitivity increases as you approach the station
Station passage marked by TO/FROM flip and needle activity
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Describe how the VOR receiver can be used to: maintain a required track from a VOR station
concept: To track outbound from a VOR, set the OBS to the desired outbound radial. Fly that radial as a heading (applying drift correction as needed) and keep the CDI centred. The TO/FROM indicator should read FROM the whole way out. Same rules apply — if the needle moves left, the course is to your left, so turn left a few degrees to intercept and hold it. As you get further from the station, the radials diverge and CDI sensitivity decreases, so heading corrections become coarser and less urgent.
exam_tip: Track from the station: set OBS to outbound radial, fly that course, keep CDI centred. FROM indication throughout. Sensitivity decreases with distance.
key_facts: Set OBS to the desired outbound radial
Fly that radial as a heading; adjust to keep CDI centred
TO/FROM should read FROM throughout
CDI sensitivity decreases as you move away from the station
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the orientation of the CDI while maintaining the required radial when drift correction is being applied
concept: When you're holding a radial with drift correction applied, your heading is NOT the same as the radial. If the radial is 090 and you have a 10-degree left crosswind component, you'll be flying heading 080 to compensate — but the CDI will be centred because you're still tracking the 090 radial. The key insight: the CDI shows displacement from the course, not displacement from your heading. You can be pointed well off the course and still be on it, as long as your track equals the course. Exam writers love this one.
exam_tip: With drift correction, heading ≠ course, but the CDI is still centred if your track = course. CDI shows track displacement, not heading displacement.
key_facts: Drift correction means heading differs from the radial being tracked
CDI shows displacement from the course, not from your heading
A centred CDI means your track equals the course, regardless of heading
Common exam scenario — understand this thoroughly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the behaviour of the CDI while the aircraft is off the selected radial, and the HDG degrees M and OBS selection are: within 90 degrees of each other
concept: When your heading and the OBS setting are within 90 degrees of each other, the CDI shows correct 'command' sense — that is, if the needle is to the left of centre, you need to turn left to intercept the course, and vice versa. This is the normal 'fly toward the needle' rule, and it works whenever you're roughly heading in the same general direction as the selected course. This is how the VOR is designed to be used, and in practice you should set the OBS so you're heading within 90 degrees of it.
exam_tip: Heading and OBS within 90 deg = normal sensing. Fly toward the needle. This is the correct setup.
key_facts: When heading and OBS are within 90 degrees, sensing is normal
CDI needle position reflects actual direction to the course
Fly toward the needle to intercept
Always set OBS so you're heading roughly toward it
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the behaviour of the CDI while the aircraft is off the selected radial, and the HDG degrees M and OBS selection are: more than 90 degrees apart
concept: If your heading and the OBS setting are more than 90 degrees apart, the sensing reverses. The CDI now shows 'reverse' sense — the needle points the wrong way. If the course is to your left, the needle shows right, and vice versa. This is why you should never track a VOR course on a heading more than 90 degrees off the OBS — if you're flying outbound and accidentally have the OBS set to the inbound course, or vice versa, you'll get reverse sensing and end up further from the course with every correction. Always set the OBS to the course you're actually trying to fly, not its reciprocal.
exam_tip: Heading and OBS more than 90 deg apart = REVERSE sensing. Fly AWAY from the needle. Better still: set the OBS correctly so you don't end up here.
key_facts: When heading and OBS are more than 90 degrees apart, sensing is reversed
CDI needle points opposite to the actual direction of the course
Flying toward the needle takes you further from the course
Always set OBS to the course you intend to fly, never its reciprocal
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Given suitable diagrams of VOR presentation (including RMI and HSI), demonstrate its use for: orientation
concept: For orientation using a diagram of a VOR (or RMI or HSI), the question is usually 'where am I relative to the station?' For a VOR indicator, read the OBS setting and whether the CDI is centred with a TO or FROM — that tells you which radial you're on or near. For an RMI, the needle points directly at the station, so the head of the needle gives you the magnetic bearing to the station and the tail gives you the radial you're on (the FROM bearing). For an HSI, read the course arrow and the deviation needle — same as a VOR indicator, just prettier. The key is to think in terms of magnetic bearings from the station, not from the aeroplane.
exam_tip: Orientation = 'what radial am I on and which side of the station?' RMI is easiest — head = to, tail = from.
key_facts: On a VOR indicator: OBS + TO/FROM + CDI centred tells you the radial
On an RMI: needle head = bearing TO station; tail = radial (bearing from)
On an HSI: same as VOR but integrated with heading card
Always think in magnetic bearings from the station
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Given suitable diagrams of VOR presentation (including RMI and HSI), demonstrate its use for: crossing a radial and station passing
concept: Crossing a radial shows up as the CDI sweeping from one side, through centre, to the other side — crisp and decisive when you're close to the station, slower when you're far away. Station passage is different: as you fly overhead the VOR, the CDI typically becomes erratic (the 'cone of confusion'), the TO/FROM indicator flips from TO to FROM, and on an RMI the needle swings rapidly through 180 degrees. The best indication of station passage is actually the first positive TO-to-FROM flip of the flag.
exam_tip: Crossing a radial: CDI sweeps smoothly through centre. Station passage: CDI erratic (cone of confusion), TO/FROM flips, RMI needle swings 180.
key_facts: Crossing a radial: CDI sweeps through centre from one side to the other
Station passage: CDI becomes erratic in the cone of confusion
Station passage marked by the first positive TO-to-FROM flip
RMI needle swings rapidly through 180 degrees at station passage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Given suitable diagrams of VOR presentation (including RMI and HSI), demonstrate its use for: intercepting radials inbound and outbound
concept: To intercept a radial inbound, set the OBS to the desired inbound course, check whether you're right or left of that course (where the CDI sits), and turn to an intercept heading typically 30–45 degrees toward the course. Fly that heading until the CDI begins to move toward centre, then lead the turn slightly and roll onto the final course as the CDI centres. Intercepting a radial outbound is the same logic with the OBS set to the outbound course. The sharper the angle, the quicker the intercept but the more aggressive the rollout; 45 degrees is a good standard.
exam_tip: Intercept angle ~30–45 degrees. Set OBS, turn toward course, fly intercept heading, lead the rollout as CDI starts moving to centre.
key_facts: Set OBS to desired course (inbound or outbound)
Check which side of course you're on
Turn 30–45 degrees toward the course
Fly intercept heading until CDI moves toward centre
Lead the rollout slightly and roll onto final course
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Discuss the factors affecting range and accuracy of VOR, including: maximum range
concept: VOR is a VHF line-of-sight system, so maximum range is determined by line-of-sight between your aeroplane and the ground station. Use the standard formula: range (nm) = 1.23 × (√h_station + √h_aircraft), with heights in feet. A typical NZ VOR at 500 ft AMSL, with you cruising at 10,000 ft, gives about 1.23 × (22 + 100) = 150 nm. At lower altitudes, range drops off sharply — at 2,000 ft you're down to about 85 nm. Above about FL200, published navaid coverage in NZ is generally adequate for en-route navigation, but outside controlled airspace you need to plan carefully in mountainous terrain where line-of-sight is lost behind ridges.
exam_tip: VOR max range = 1.23 × (√h_aircraft + √h_station) in nm, with heights in feet. It's line-of-sight — terrain and altitude matter.
key_facts: VOR is a VHF line-of-sight system
Max range = 1.23 × (√h_station + √h_aircraft) nm, heights in feet
Range increases with altitude
Terrain can shadow the signal at any altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Discuss the factors affecting range and accuracy of VOR, including: published route operating limitations
concept: Each published airway segment or arrival/departure procedure in the AIP NZ has its own minimum altitudes and coverage limits that account for VOR range, terrain shadowing and accuracy. The MEA (Minimum Enroute Altitude) guarantees usable navaid coverage along the route; the MOCA (Minimum Obstacle Clearance Altitude) guarantees obstacle clearance but only guarantees navaid coverage within 22 nm of the navaid. Flying below MEA but above MOCA is legal in some cases but you may lose positive navigation between stations. Always respect the published limits — they're there because someone has already done the line-of-sight and interference analysis for you.
exam_tip: MEA = obstacle clearance AND navaid coverage along the route. MOCA = obstacle clearance + navaid coverage within 22 nm of the station.
key_facts: MEA (Minimum Enroute Altitude) guarantees navaid coverage along the route
MOCA (Minimum Obstacle Clearance Altitude) guarantees coverage only within 22 nm of the navaid
Published route limits account for terrain, range and interference
Always comply with published minima
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: Discuss the factors affecting range and accuracy of VOR, including: errors, particularly terrain effect error with scalloping and radial bending
concept: VOR errors come in a few flavours. Instrument error is the receiver/indicator's own inaccuracy — typically a degree or two. Site error is built into the station installation and varies with bearing. Scalloping is a cyclic oscillation of the CDI caused by reflections from terrain or structures near the station — the needle wags back and forth around the true radial as the reflections interfere with the direct signal. Radial bending is a more persistent distortion of the radial caused by terrain effects — the radial isn't a perfectly straight line out from the station, but bends slightly in regions where terrain or obstacles affect propagation. Both scalloping and bending are much worse in mountainous terrain, which makes a big chunk of NZ interesting for VOR work. Conventional VORs (CVOR) are more susceptible than Doppler VORs (DVOR), which is why most NZ VORs are now DVOR.
exam_tip: Terrain effect: scalloping (cyclic needle wagging from reflections) and radial bending (persistent distortion). Worse in mountains. DVOR reduces both.
key_facts: Instrument error: inherent inaccuracy of the receiver (typically 1–2 degrees)
Site error: fixed error built into the station installation
Scalloping: cyclic CDI oscillation caused by terrain reflections
Radial bending: persistent distortion of the radial from terrain effects
Doppler VOR (DVOR) is much less susceptible than Conventional VOR (CVOR)
NZ's terrain makes many regions prone to terrain-induced errors
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard VOR theory

syllabus_desc: State the primary functions of the DME
concept: DME (Distance Measuring Equipment) does exactly one thing: it tells you how far you are from the DME ground station in nautical miles. It's a secondary radar system at heart — your airborne DME interrogator transmits pulse pairs to the ground transponder, which replies, and your set measures the round-trip time to calculate slant range. Modern DMEs also derive ground speed and time-to-station from the rate at which the distance is changing. That's it: distance, ground speed and time-to-station. Simple, and incredibly useful.
