exam_tip: DME primary function = slant range distance from the station in nm. Also derives ground speed and time-to-station.
key_facts: Provides slant range distance to the DME ground station in nautical miles
Derives ground speed from the rate of distance change
Derives time-to-station from distance and ground speed
A secondary (cooperative) radar system
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard DME theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic principle of operation of DME
concept: The airborne DME interrogator fires randomly spaced pulse pairs at the ground station on the interrogation frequency. The ground transponder receives them and, after a fixed known delay (called the transponder delay or reply delay, typically 50 microseconds), replies on a different frequency with its own pulse pairs. Your airborne set uses the random spacing of your own interrogations as a 'fingerprint' to recognise its own replies among all the replies going to other aeroplanes using the same station. Time the round-trip, subtract the known transponder delay, divide by two for one-way, multiply by the speed of light, and you have the slant range.
exam_tip: DME: airborne pulse pairs → ground transponder → reply with fixed delay → airborne set times round trip, subtracts delay, calculates slant range.
key_facts: Airborne interrogator transmits pulse pairs on a unique random pattern
Ground transponder replies after a fixed known delay (typically 50 microseconds)
Random pulse spacing lets the airborne set recognise its own replies
Round-trip time minus the delay gives slant range
Works at UHF frequencies (962–1213 MHz)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard DME theory

syllabus_desc: Describe current DME presentations
concept: Modern DME presentation is usually digital — a three- or four-digit display showing nautical miles to the station, alongside a ground speed display (kt) and time-to-station (minutes). It's integrated into the radio stack or the EFIS/PFD in glass-cockpit machines. Older DMEs had round mechanical drum counters, but you'll mostly see digital readouts now. On a PFD the DME is presented as a small numerical box in a corner of the navigation display, often right next to the tuned VOR/ILS identifier.
exam_tip: Modern DME: digital nm display plus ground speed and time-to-station. Integrated into the radio stack or EFIS.
key_facts: Digital display of distance in nautical miles
Usually shows ground speed and time-to-station alongside distance
Integrated into the radio stack or EFIS/PFD in modern aircraft
Older installations used mechanical drum-counter displays
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard DME theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of station identification before using the DME
concept: Same rule as VOR: don't use a DME without identifying it first. The Morse ident is transmitted every 30 seconds or so on the associated VOR or ILS frequency (DMEs are usually paired with a VOR or ILS, and you hear both idents — the VOR ident more frequently, followed by the DME ident at a slightly different tone). If the DME is unserviceable or under maintenance, the ident won't be present, and any distance reading displayed is unreliable. No ident, no nav.
exam_tip: DME ident is slightly different tone from the paired VOR/ILS and comes every ~30 seconds. No ident = don't use the DME.
key_facts: DME Morse ident is transmitted alongside the paired VOR/ILS ident
DME ident has a slightly different audio tone from the VOR/ILS
Transmitted roughly every 30 seconds
No ident means the DME is not usable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard DME theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a typical DME controller, including: tuning with a paired VOR or ILS frequency
concept: Most DMEs are paired with a VOR or ILS — the two facilities share a location (or are very close) and the frequencies are locked together: when you tune the VOR/ILS frequency, the DME automatically tunes to its paired frequency. So to use the DME co-located with the Hamilton VOR, you just tune 114.0 MHz (HN) on your VOR/NAV radio and the DME comes along for the ride. Same for an ILS/DME — tune the ILS frequency, get the DME automatically.
exam_tip: Tune the paired VOR/ILS frequency and the DME auto-tunes. You don't need to set the DME separately in the co-located case.
key_facts: DMEs are often paired with VOR or ILS facilities
Tuning the VOR/ILS frequency automatically tunes the paired DME
The pilot doesn't need to tune the DME separately in this case
Paired frequencies are published in the AIP NZ and on charts
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard DME theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a typical DME controller, including: tuning directly to a DME frequency
concept: Some DMEs aren't paired with a VOR or ILS — these are stand-alone DMEs, and you have to tune them directly by entering the DME frequency (or the paired pseudo-frequency) into the NAV or DME control. The AIP NZ lists stand-alone DMEs with the frequency or channel to use. In NZ you'll find a few of these around certain aerodromes where a DME is needed for an approach or arrival route without a co-located VOR.
exam_tip: Stand-alone DMEs need to be tuned directly — enter the DME frequency or channel manually. Check AIP NZ for the tuning details.
key_facts: Stand-alone DMEs are not paired with VOR or ILS
Must be tuned directly on the DME control
Listed in AIP NZ with frequency or channel information
Used where a DME is needed without a co-located VOR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard DME theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a typical DME controller, including: DME ident
concept: DME ident is your verification. On the audio selector panel, select the NAV or DME audio and listen for the Morse ident that's transmitted on the same frequency as the paired VOR/ILS (or on its own if it's a stand-alone DME). The DME ident is usually noticeably different in tone from the VOR or ILS ident, so you can tell them apart. Listen for both if you're using a combined VOR/DME — you want to confirm both are operational. If you only hear the VOR ident and not the DME ident, the DME may be unserviceable.
exam_tip: Listen for the DME ident separately — it's a different tone from the VOR/ILS ident. Hearing the VOR ident alone doesn't confirm the DME.
key_facts: DME ident is a Morse code identification tone
Different pitch from the paired VOR or ILS ident
Both idents should be heard for a co-located VOR/DME or ILS/DME
Missing DME ident means the DME may be unserviceable even if the VOR is fine
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard DME theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a typical DME controller, including: indication of signal loss
concept: When the DME loses signal — either because you're out of coverage, terrain has shadowed the station, or the station itself has failed — the display will typically show dashes, 'NO COMP', 'FAIL' or blank out, depending on the unit. A momentary loss during turns (antenna shadowing by the fuselage) is common and the set usually recovers within a few seconds. A persistent loss in good coverage conditions means a problem — either with the station or with your equipment.
exam_tip: Signal loss = dashes, FAIL or blank. Brief dropouts in turns are normal; persistent loss means a real problem.
key_facts: Signal loss shown as dashes, FAIL flag, or blank display
Brief losses during turns are normal (fuselage shadowing)
Persistent loss in good conditions indicates a station or equipment problem
Display should always be cross-checked against other nav aids
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard DME theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a typical DME controller, including: saturation
concept: DME saturation occurs when a single ground transponder is being interrogated by too many aircraft at once and can't reply to all of them. The transponder has a maximum reply rate — once saturated, it starts refusing replies to weaker signals (typically the more distant aircraft). The airborne set either loses lock or shows intermittent readings. It's rare at most NZ DMEs because there aren't enough aircraft to saturate them, but at busy terminal-area DMEs in high-traffic periods it can happen. The solution is to use another DME if available, or accept the degraded service.
exam_tip: Saturation = ground transponder overloaded by too many interrogations. Weaker (more distant) aircraft lose service first. Rare in NZ.
key_facts: Saturation occurs when the ground DME is overloaded with interrogations
The transponder drops replies to weaker signals first — typically distant aircraft
Airborne receiver may lose lock or show intermittent indications
Rare in NZ except at busy terminal-area DMEs
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard DME theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the following: DME design maximum range
concept: DME operates in the 962–1213 MHz UHF band, which is line-of-sight. Design maximum range is around 200 nm, but in practice it's limited by line-of-sight between the aircraft and the station. The same formula applies as for any line-of-sight navaid: range = 1.23 × (√h_station + √h_aircraft) in nm with heights in feet. So at typical IFR cruise levels you'll get DME out to about 150–200 nm from a good ground station, and less at lower altitudes.
exam_tip: DME design max range: ~200 nm. Limited in practice by line-of-sight: range = 1.23 × (√h_aircraft + √h_station) nm.
key_facts: DME operates at UHF (962–1213 MHz), which is line-of-sight
Design maximum range is around 200 nm
Practical range limited by line-of-sight between aircraft and station
Formula: range (nm) = 1.23 × (√h_station + √h_aircraft), heights in feet
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard DME theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the following: DME distance (slant range) versus horizontal range
concept: DME gives you slant range, not horizontal range. It measures the straight-line distance from your aeroplane to the ground station — so if you're directly over the station at 6,000 ft, the DME reads 1 nm, not zero. At long horizontal distances the slant range is very close to the horizontal distance (because the height component is small compared to the horizontal), but close to the station it's noticeably different. As a rule of thumb, slant range roughly equals horizontal range when you're more than about 10× your altitude away horizontally; inside that, correct for altitude if accuracy matters. Overhead the station, minimum DME reading equals your height in nm (roughly 1 nm per 6,000 ft).
exam_tip: DME is slant range. Overhead the station at 6,000 ft = 1 nm DME reading. Error is negligible far from station, significant close in.
key_facts: DME measures the straight-line (slant) distance from aircraft to station
Differs from horizontal distance by the height component
Significant error only when you're close to the station
Overhead the station: minimum reading is approximately 1 nm per 6,000 ft of height
Rule of thumb: slant ≈ horizontal when horizontal > 10× altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard DME theory

syllabus_desc: Calculate the expected maximum range of the DME at different altitudes
concept: To calculate the maximum expected DME range, use the standard line-of-sight formula: range (nm) = 1.23 × (√h_aircraft + √h_station), with heights in feet. A few worked examples: cruising at FL100 (10,000 ft) to a station at 500 ft AMSL: range = 1.23 × (√10,000 + √500) = 1.23 × (100 + 22) = ~150 nm. At FL200 (20,000 ft): 1.23 × (141 + 22) = ~200 nm. At 5,000 ft: 1.23 × (71 + 22) = ~114 nm. At 2,000 ft: 1.23 × (45 + 22) = ~82 nm. Remember: this is the theoretical line-of-sight limit; terrain shadowing or station power limits can reduce it below this. The published service volume in the AIP NZ is what matters operationally.
exam_tip: Practice the formula. At 10,000 ft to a station at 500 ft: ~150 nm. At 20,000 ft: ~200 nm. Always heights in feet for this formula.
key_facts: Formula: range (nm) = 1.23 × (√h_aircraft + √h_station), heights in feet
At FL100 to a low-level station: approximately 150 nm
At FL200: approximately 200 nm
At 5,000 ft: approximately 114 nm
Published service volumes in AIP NZ are the operational limits
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR 4.1; standard DME theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of an ILS, including: localiser principles
concept: The localiser gives you lateral guidance to the runway centreline. A transmitter at the far end of the runway radiates two overlapping lobes — one modulated at 90 Hz, the other at 150 Hz — aimed down the approach path. On the extended runway centreline the two lobes are perfectly equal in depth of modulation. Off to the right of centreline (looking from the aeroplane toward the runway), the 150 Hz signal is stronger; off to the left, the 90 Hz is stronger. Your receiver compares the depth of modulation of the two signals and produces a proportional deviation, which drives the CDI left or right.
exam_tip: Localiser: 90 Hz left, 150 Hz right (looking toward the runway). Equal modulation = on centreline. CDI proportional to the difference.
key_facts: Transmitter at the far end of the runway
Two overlapping signal lobes modulated at 90 Hz and 150 Hz
On centreline the modulation depths are equal
150 Hz dominates to the right, 90 Hz to the left (looking toward the runway)
Receiver produces lateral deviation from the difference in depths
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR and AD charts; standard ILS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of an ILS, including: standard rated coverage
concept: The standard rated coverage of an ILS localiser is typically 25 nm within 10 degrees either side of the front course, and 17 nm within 35 degrees either side. Within those volumes the signal is guaranteed to be usable. Outside them, you can still pick up a signal but you cannot rely on its accuracy for approach guidance. The glideslope has its own coverage volume, generally 10 nm inside the localiser volume. AIP NZ specifies the exact coverage for each installation; non-standard values are highlighted.
exam_tip: Standard ILS coverage: localiser ≈ 25 nm within 10 deg, 17 nm within 35 deg of course. Glideslope is usable out to about 10 nm.
key_facts: Localiser: 25 nm within 10 degrees of front course
Localiser: 17 nm within 35 degrees of front course
Glideslope: approximately 10 nm, centred on the centreline
Outside rated coverage, signals must not be used for approach guidance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR and AD charts; standard ILS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of an ILS, including: CDI indication
concept: The localiser CDI looks like a VOR CDI but behaves very differently. It's fed directly by the localiser receiver and shows lateral displacement from the runway centreline. On a fixed or rotatable card or an HSI, it's the same needle — but the sensitivity is much higher than a VOR. Full-scale deflection is typically ±2.5 degrees (compared to ±10 degrees for a VOR), so the needle moves sharply for small lateral errors. The same 'fly toward the needle' rule applies: needle left means the centreline is to your left. Unlike a VOR, there's no OBS — you can't select a localiser course, it's fixed by the physical geometry of the runway.
exam_tip: Localiser CDI: same display as VOR but 4× more sensitive. No OBS — course is fixed by runway geometry. Fly toward the needle.
key_facts: Same needle as a VOR CDI but driven by localiser signals
Full-scale deflection is typically ±2.5 degrees (versus ±10 degrees for VOR)
Much more sensitive than VOR — small deviations produce large needle movement
Course is fixed by runway alignment — no OBS selection
Fly toward the needle to correct back to centreline
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR and AD charts; standard ILS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of an ILS, including: glideslope principles
concept: The glideslope gives you vertical guidance along the approach path. A transmitter near the touchdown zone (offset to one side of the runway for physical reasons) radiates two overlapping lobes in the vertical plane — 90 Hz above the glideslope, 150 Hz below it. On the correct glideslope the modulation depths are equal; above the slope the 90 Hz dominates, below it the 150 Hz dominates. Your receiver produces a proportional vertical deviation which drives the glideslope needle up or down on the indicator.
exam_tip: Glideslope: 90 Hz above the slope, 150 Hz below. Equal modulation = on slope. Needle goes down if you're high, up if you're low.
key_facts: Transmitter near the touchdown zone, offset from the runway centreline
Two overlapping lobes in the vertical plane: 90 Hz above, 150 Hz below the slope
On slope: modulation depths equal
Above slope: 90 Hz dominates
Below slope: 150 Hz dominates
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR and AD charts; standard ILS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of an ILS, including: glideslope angle
concept: The standard glideslope angle is 3 degrees. Some installations in challenging terrain use steeper angles — 3.5 or even 3.77 degrees at a few NZ aerodromes — to give obstacle clearance on the approach. The glideslope angle is always published on the approach plate and in the AIP NZ approach chart. A steeper angle means a higher descent rate for a given ground speed, and generally a more demanding approach; plan accordingly.
exam_tip: Standard glideslope: 3 degrees. Check the approach plate for non-standard values. Steeper slope = higher descent rate for the same ground speed.
key_facts: Standard glideslope angle is 3 degrees
Some NZ installations use steeper angles (3.5 or higher) for obstacle clearance
Angle is published on the approach plate and in AIP NZ
Steeper angles require higher descent rates
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR and AD charts; standard ILS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of an ILS, including: glideslope indications
concept: The glideslope indicator on a VOR/ILS instrument is a small horizontal needle that moves up and down against a scale of dots. Centred = on slope. Needle down (below centre) = you're above the slope, you need to descend more. Needle up (above centre) = you're below the slope, you need to descend less (or add power). On an HSI the glideslope pointer typically appears to the right or left of the heading display. In a PFD the glideslope is shown as a magenta diamond moving against a vertical scale on the side of the attitude display.
exam_tip: Glideslope needle: down = you're high, up = you're low. Fly toward the needle, just like the localiser.
key_facts: Horizontal needle moving against a vertical scale
Centred: on slope
Needle below centre: aircraft is above the slope
Needle above centre: aircraft is below the slope
On HSIs and PFDs, presentation varies but the principle is the same
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR and AD charts; standard ILS theory

syllabus_desc: State the localiser and glideslope displacement represented by full scale deviation of the CDI and glideslope indicators
concept: Full-scale CDI deflection on the localiser corresponds to approximately ±2.5 degrees from centreline. At the threshold, that's about ±150 metres (500 ft). Full-scale deflection on the glideslope corresponds to approximately ±0.7 degrees from the slope — much tighter than the localiser, because vertical accuracy matters more. At 1 nm from touchdown on a 3-degree slope, ±0.7 degrees is about ±80 ft vertically. Half-scale deflection means you're about half those values off. Note how tight the tolerances are — this is why flying an ILS demands fine control inputs.
exam_tip: Localiser full-scale = ±2.5 deg. Glideslope full-scale = ±0.7 deg. Glideslope is nearly 4× more sensitive than localiser.
key_facts: Localiser full-scale CDI deflection: ±2.5 degrees from centreline
Glideslope full-scale deflection: ±0.7 degrees from the slope
At the threshold, localiser full-scale ≈ ±150 m
Glideslope is much more sensitive than localiser
Half-scale deflection indicates half those values
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR and AD charts; standard ILS theory

syllabus_desc: State the rule-of-thumb methods of calculating: the required height on a 3 degree glideslope at a given distance from the threshold
concept: The rule of thumb for the height you should be on a 3-degree glideslope at any distance from the touchdown zone is 300 ft per nm. So at 3 nm out you should be at roughly 900 ft above touchdown elevation, at 5 nm you should be at 1,500 ft, at 10 nm you should be at 3,000 ft. Add the touchdown zone elevation to get your altitude AMSL. It's a quick sanity check to make sure the glideslope signal you're seeing is actually the real one and not a false glideslope (yes, they exist — more on that in a moment).
exam_tip: 3-degree glideslope: 300 ft per nm above touchdown elevation. At 3 nm = 900 ft, at 5 nm = 1,500 ft, at 10 nm = 3,000 ft.
key_facts: Rule of thumb: 300 ft per nm above touchdown elevation on a 3-degree slope
At 3 nm: approximately 900 ft AAL
At 5 nm: approximately 1,500 ft AAL
At 10 nm: approximately 3,000 ft AAL
Add touchdown zone elevation for altitude AMSL
Use as a sanity check against false glideslope signals
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR and AD charts; standard ILS theory

syllabus_desc: State the rule-of-thumb methods of calculating: the rate of descent required to maintain a 3 degree glideslope at any given groundspeed
concept: The rule of thumb for the rate of descent required to hold a 3-degree glideslope is: ground speed (kt) × 5 = vertical speed (fpm). So at 90 kt ground speed you need 450 fpm down; at 120 kt you need 600; at 140 kt you need 700. For a steeper slope (say 3.5 degrees), multiply ground speed by about 6 instead. This is rough but remarkably accurate for flying an ILS — plug in your ground speed (not your airspeed!) and you'll get the right ballpark descent rate. Then fine-tune to keep the glideslope needle centred.
exam_tip: 3-degree glideslope ROD: GS × 5 in fpm. At 120 kt GS = 600 fpm. For 3.5 degree slope, multiply by 6.
key_facts: Rule of thumb: VSI (fpm) = ground speed (kt) × 5 for a 3-degree slope
At 90 kt GS: approximately 450 fpm descent
At 120 kt GS: approximately 600 fpm descent
At 140 kt GS: approximately 700 fpm descent
For steeper slopes (3.5 deg), multiply ground speed by ~6
Use ground speed, not airspeed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR and AD charts; standard ILS theory

syllabus_desc: Given suitable diagrams of instrument presentation, interpret aircraft position with respect to ILS centreline and glideslope
concept: Interpreting ILS presentations is all about 'fly toward the needle'. Localiser needle left: centreline is left, turn left to correct. Localiser needle right: centreline is right. Glideslope needle up: the slope is above you — reduce descent (or increase power) to climb back up to it. Glideslope needle down: the slope is below you — increase descent to intercept it from above. On a standard VOR/ILS indicator the two needles form a crosshairs; on an HSI or PFD the presentation is integrated with heading. Either way, aim to put the crosshairs at centre and keep them there with small, smooth corrections. If both needles are full scale the opposite way from where you are (pegged), you're well off the approach and may need to break off and try again.
exam_tip: Localiser needle left/right: turn toward it. Glideslope needle up = you're low, needle down = you're high. Fly toward the needles, always.
key_facts: Localiser needle shows lateral displacement — fly toward the needle
Glideslope needle shows vertical displacement — fly toward the needle
Needle up = aircraft is below the slope (need to climb or reduce descent)
Needle down = aircraft is above the slope (need to increase descent)
Both needles fully deflected: aircraft is significantly off the approach
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR and AD charts; standard ILS theory

syllabus_desc: Describe in basic terms the principal GNSS systems -- GPS, Galileo, GLONASS and BeiDou
concept: GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) is the umbrella term for the various satellite-based navigation systems in operation. GPS is the American system — the oldest and most widely used, with around 31 operational satellites. GLONASS is the Russian equivalent, with 24 satellites in a different orbit configuration. Galileo is the European system, fully operational since 2016 with 24+ satellites and designed from the start for civil use with higher accuracy. BeiDou is the Chinese system, fully operational globally since 2020. All four systems work on the same basic principles — transmitting timing signals from satellites in known orbits — and modern multi-constellation receivers can use signals from all of them simultaneously for improved accuracy and availability.
exam_tip: Four main GNSS systems: GPS (USA), GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (Europe), BeiDou (China). Modern receivers use multiple constellations.
key_facts: GNSS is the umbrella term for satellite navigation systems
GPS — USA, around 31 satellites, oldest system
GLONASS — Russia, 24 satellites
Galileo — Europe, 24+ satellites, civil-focused
BeiDou — China, fully global since 2020
Multi-constellation receivers can use all four for improved accuracy and integrity
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the three main segments of the GNSS (GPS) system
concept: GPS (and GNSS generally) consists of three segments. The space segment is the satellite constellation itself — the satellites orbiting Earth transmitting signals. The control segment is the network of ground stations that monitor the satellites, calculate their orbits and clock corrections, and upload updates to the satellites. The user segment is everything on the receiving end — your airborne GPS receiver, your phone, your car navigation, any device that picks up GPS signals and uses them to calculate position.
exam_tip: Three segments: Space (satellites), Control (ground monitoring), User (receivers). Know all three.
key_facts: Space segment: the satellite constellation transmitting signals
Control segment: ground stations monitoring and controlling the satellites
User segment: receivers that use the signals to compute position
All three segments are essential for GNSS to function
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Outline the elements of the control segment
concept: The control segment consists of a master control station, several monitor stations distributed around the world, and ground antennas for uploading data. Monitor stations continuously track the satellites' positions, analyse their signals, and send data back to the master control station. The master station calculates precise orbit and clock corrections and uploads them to the satellites via the ground antennas. This keeps each satellite's broadcast ephemeris (its self-reported orbital position) accurate — without the control segment, the satellites' position data would slowly drift and GNSS accuracy would degrade.
exam_tip: Control segment = master control station + monitor stations + ground upload antennas. Tracks satellites, calculates corrections, uploads updates.
key_facts: Master control station coordinates the whole ground operation
Monitor stations track satellite signals and positions worldwide
Ground antennas upload corrections to the satellites
Keeps broadcast ephemeris data accurate
Without the control segment, accuracy would degrade over time
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the user segment, including the basic principle of satellite ranging
concept: The user segment includes your airborne GPS receiver and its antenna. Satellite ranging works by measuring the time taken for a signal to travel from a satellite to your receiver. Each satellite continuously transmits a precise timing code along with its known position in space. Your receiver measures the time delay between when the signal was transmitted (encoded in the signal) and when it arrived, multiplies by the speed of light, and gets the range to that satellite. Do this for multiple satellites simultaneously and you can solve for your 3D position.
exam_tip: User segment = your receiver. Ranging = measure signal travel time × speed of light. One satellite = one range (a sphere around it).
key_facts: User segment includes the airborne receiver and antenna
Each satellite transmits precise timing codes and its known position
Range to a satellite = signal travel time × speed of light
Each range defines a sphere around that satellite
Multiple ranges intersect to give a position
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the principles of fixing position using the GNSS system; including: the number of satellites required for 2D and 3D fixing
concept: For a 2D fix (latitude and longitude only, with altitude assumed or derived from another source), you need at least 3 satellites. Three range measurements give three intersecting spheres, and the point where they cross is your position in the plane. For a true 3D fix (latitude, longitude and altitude) you need at least 4 satellites — the fourth measurement is needed because of the receiver clock error. The receiver's internal clock is not perfectly synchronised with the satellites' atomic clocks, and the fourth satellite lets the receiver solve for that clock offset alongside the three spatial coordinates.
exam_tip: 2D fix = 3 satellites. 3D fix = 4 satellites. The fourth is needed to solve the receiver clock error.
key_facts: 2D fix (lat/long): minimum 3 satellites
3D fix (lat/long/altitude): minimum 4 satellites
The fourth satellite is needed to solve the receiver clock offset
More satellites = more redundancy and better accuracy
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the principles of fixing position using the GNSS system; including: the operation of RAIM
concept: RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring) is the receiver's ability to check its own answer for consistency. With more satellites than the minimum needed for a fix, the receiver can compare different combinations of satellites and see if they all agree on a position. If they do, integrity is good. If one satellite is giving bad data, the results won't agree and the receiver can flag the problem. RAIM is what makes GPS usable for IFR — without it, you can't trust the fix enough for primary navigation. The receiver needs at least 5 satellites visible with good geometry for RAIM to work.
exam_tip: RAIM = Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring. Checks consistency between extra satellites. Minimum 5 satellites with good geometry for fault detection.
key_facts: RAIM = Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring
Compares solutions from different combinations of satellites
Detects faulty satellite data by checking consistency
Requires more satellites than needed for the basic fix
Essential for IFR GPS navigation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the principles of fixing position using the GNSS system; including: the number of satellites required for fault detection (FD) and for fault detection and exclusion (FDE)
concept: Fault Detection (FD) requires at least 5 satellites with good geometry — the 4 needed for a 3D fix plus 1 extra for integrity checking. With 5 satellites, the receiver can tell you that one of them is wrong, but it can't identify which one. Fault Detection and Exclusion (FDE) requires 6 satellites — 5 for FD plus 1 more to identify and exclude the faulty satellite from the solution. FDE is needed for oceanic and remote area operations where no backup navigation is available; FD is sufficient for most other IFR operations.
exam_tip: Fault Detection = 5 satellites. Fault Detection and Exclusion = 6 satellites. FDE is needed where there's no backup nav (oceanic/remote).
key_facts: Fault Detection (FD): minimum 5 satellites with good geometry
FD can detect a fault but cannot identify which satellite is faulty
Fault Detection and Exclusion (FDE): minimum 6 satellites
FDE can identify and remove the faulty satellite from the solution
FDE is required for oceanic and remote area operations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the principles of fixing position using the GNSS system; including: barometric aiding
concept: Barometric aiding uses altitude input from the aircraft's altimeter as a substitute for one of the satellite measurements. If you only have 3 or 4 satellites visible (not enough for a good 3D fix with RAIM), the receiver can use the pressure altitude as a known vertical coordinate and solve for lat/long and clock error with fewer satellites. This is useful in situations where satellite coverage is briefly degraded, but it introduces some dependence on the altimeter being correct — which is why many IFR GPS receivers incorporate barometric aiding as a standard feature.
exam_tip: Barometric aiding = use altimeter altitude as a substitute for one satellite measurement. Reduces the satellite count needed but adds altimeter dependence.
key_facts: Uses pressure altitude from the altimeter as a known input
Substitutes for one satellite measurement in the position solution
Allows navigation with fewer visible satellites
Introduces dependence on altimeter accuracy
Common feature in IFR GPS receivers
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain how the receiver predicts the position of various satellites
concept: The receiver predicts where each satellite should be at any given moment using two data sources it gets from the satellites themselves. The almanac is a low-accuracy description of the orbits of all satellites in the constellation — each satellite broadcasts almanac data for every other satellite, so your receiver can find them. The ephemeris is a high-accuracy orbit description for each individual satellite, broadcast only by that satellite, updated by the control segment every few hours. Your receiver uses the almanac to know roughly where to look for satellites, then uses the ephemeris data to know exactly where each visible satellite is when it calculates ranges. Ephemeris data has a finite validity (typically 4 hours) — beyond that, accuracy degrades.
exam_tip: Almanac = rough orbits of all satellites (broadcast by every satellite). Ephemeris = precise orbit for a specific satellite (broadcast only by that satellite, updated every few hours).
key_facts: Almanac: rough orbit data for all satellites, broadcast by every satellite
Ephemeris: precise orbit data for a specific satellite
Ephemeris is updated by the control segment every few hours
Ephemeris validity is typically around 4 hours
Receiver uses almanac to find satellites and ephemeris for accurate positioning
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: State the sources of GNSS error, and the maximum error which can be expected
concept: GNSS errors come from several sources. Ephemeris errors are small inaccuracies in the satellite's reported orbit position. Satellite clock errors are small timing errors at the satellite. Ionospheric delays bend and slow the signal as it passes through the ionosphere, varying with solar activity and the signal's path angle. Tropospheric delays do the same in the lower atmosphere due to water vapour. Multipath errors come from the signal reflecting off surfaces (aircraft fuselage, terrain) before reaching the antenna. Receiver noise and hardware errors are small electrical effects. Without any augmentation, the total maximum horizontal error in an unaugmented civilian GPS signal is generally around 15–30 metres, and most of the time much less than that — but that's the figure the exam usually wants.
exam_tip: Error sources: ephemeris, satellite clock, ionosphere, troposphere, multipath, receiver noise. Unaugmented GPS max horizontal error: around 15–30 m.
key_facts: Ephemeris errors: small inaccuracies in broadcast satellite orbits
Satellite clock errors: small timing offsets at the satellite
Ionospheric delay: signal slowing through the ionosphere
Tropospheric delay: signal slowing through the lower atmosphere
Multipath: reflections reaching the antenna via longer paths
Receiver noise: small errors in the airborne equipment
Maximum horizontal error of unaugmented civilian GPS: around 15–30 m
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: State the reasons for the display of a RAIM warning message, and the requirements under CAA Rules for continued navigation
concept: A RAIM warning appears when the receiver cannot confirm the integrity of its position solution — usually because not enough satellites are visible, the geometry is poor, or the receiver has detected inconsistency between satellite data. When RAIM is lost, the displayed position may still be correct, but you can't trust it for navigation. Under CAR Part 91 and 19, if you lose RAIM during an IFR flight, you must revert to another approved navigation method — ground-based navaids, DR, or vectors from ATC — and you cannot use GNSS as primary navigation until integrity is restored. For RNAV and RNP approaches, loss of RAIM requires you to break off the approach and either try again or use an alternative procedure.
exam_tip: RAIM warning = cannot trust GPS for nav. Must revert to ground-based aids or ATC vectors. Must break off RNAV/RNP approaches if RAIM is lost.
key_facts: RAIM warning displayed when integrity cannot be verified
Causes: insufficient satellites, poor geometry, detected inconsistency
Required to revert to alternative navigation when RAIM is lost
For RNAV/RNP approaches, loss of RAIM requires aborting the approach
Cannot use GNSS as primary navigation until integrity is restored
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of: a RAIM prediction service
concept: A RAIM prediction service lets you check whether RAIM will be available along your planned route and at your destination at the planned time. This is a web- or app-based service that uses knowledge of the satellite constellation (their orbits and any expected outages) to predict which satellites will be visible and whether their geometry will support RAIM at each waypoint. In NZ you'd typically use Airways NZ's prediction service or a similar commercial tool. For IFR flights with GNSS as a primary navigation source, you're required to check RAIM availability before departure — particularly for RNAV or RNP approaches at the destination.
exam_tip: RAIM prediction service = preflight check that RAIM will be available along your route at the planned time. Required for IFR GNSS ops.
key_facts: Web or app-based service for preflight RAIM availability checks
Uses constellation almanacs and known outages to predict availability
NZ: Airways NZ or equivalent commercial services
Required preflight for IFR GNSS navigation
Especially important for RNAV and RNP approach planning
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of: onboard RAIM alerting
concept: Onboard RAIM alerting is the live warning system in your GPS receiver that fires if RAIM is lost or degraded in flight. When RAIM is unavailable or the integrity check fails, the receiver displays a warning (typically a 'RAIM' or 'INTEG' flag) and may disable the navigation output or at least flag it as not for use. The warning is usually accompanied by an annunciator light and, on many integrated installations, an aural alert. You must treat any RAIM warning as a direction to revert to an alternative navigation source immediately.
exam_tip: Onboard RAIM alerting = real-time warning in the cockpit if RAIM fails. Always act on it — revert to alternative nav.
key_facts: Live warning system built into IFR-approved GPS receivers
Triggers when RAIM cannot be verified or integrity fails
Displayed as an on-screen warning, flag or light
May be accompanied by aural alerts on integrated installations
Requires pilot action to revert to alternative navigation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Describe the application of the WGS 84 datum, and the likely effects on the GPS display of using coordinates from another datum
concept: GPS positions are calculated in the WGS 84 datum — a global geodetic reference system that defines the shape of the Earth and the coordinate system. Aviation charts and databases worldwide are referenced to WGS 84, so the coordinates you get from GPS match the coordinates on the chart. If you use coordinates from a different datum (an old chart using a local datum, for example), there can be errors of tens to hundreds of metres between what the GPS thinks is a given lat/long and where that point actually is on the other datum. NZ historically used NZGD49 (New Zealand Geodetic Datum 1949), which differs from WGS 84 by a small but measurable amount. All NZ aviation charts and waypoints are now WGS 84, so in practice you won't encounter the issue — but you need to know what would happen if you did.
exam_tip: GPS uses WGS 84. Aviation charts use WGS 84. Mixing datums = errors of tens to hundreds of metres. Don't mix datums.
key_facts: GPS positions are calculated in the WGS 84 datum
All current aviation charts and databases are referenced to WGS 84
Using coordinates from another datum causes position errors
Errors can be tens to hundreds of metres
NZ aviation data is all WGS 84 now
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the methods of the augmentation of GPS accuracy
concept: GNSS augmentation improves accuracy and integrity beyond what the basic constellation provides. There are three main types. SBAS (Satellite-Based Augmentation Systems) like WAAS (US), EGNOS (Europe), and MSAS (Japan) use geostationary satellites to broadcast correction data that regional receivers apply to improve accuracy to around 1–3 metres. NZ doesn't have operational SBAS coverage yet, though it's coming. GBAS (Ground-Based Augmentation Systems) use local ground stations near an aerodrome to provide high-accuracy corrections for precision approaches — it's essentially a local correction service rather than a wide-area one. ABAS (Aircraft-Based Augmentation) includes RAIM and barometric aiding — the receiver uses its own resources to improve integrity, without needing external corrections. All three augmentations can operate simultaneously in modern receivers.
exam_tip: Three augmentation types: SBAS (wide-area satellite corrections), GBAS (local ground-based), ABAS (receiver-based including RAIM).
key_facts: SBAS: Satellite-Based Augmentation (WAAS, EGNOS, MSAS) for wide-area corrections
SBAS improves accuracy to around 1–3 m
GBAS: Ground-Based Augmentation for high-accuracy approaches
ABAS: Aircraft-Based Augmentation (RAIM, barometric aiding)
NZ does not currently have operational SBAS coverage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors influencing GNSS integrity, continuity and availability, including: data base validity
concept: Database validity is a major factor in GNSS integrity. Your navigation database (waypoints, airways, approaches, aerodromes) has an AIRAC cycle — 28 days — during which it's current. Flying with an out-of-date database risks using waypoint coordinates or procedures that have changed since publication, which can put you in the wrong place at the wrong time. Under CAR Part 91, IFR flights using GNSS must have a current database. Some databases allow a limited grace period if a waypoint hasn't changed, but the safest policy is to update every AIRAC cycle — many operators now do this automatically over the air or via USB updates.
exam_tip: Nav database must be current. AIRAC cycle = 28 days. Out-of-date database = risk of using wrong coordinates or outdated procedures.
key_facts: Navigation databases follow the 28-day AIRAC cycle
Waypoints, airways, approaches and aerodromes may change each cycle
IFR GNSS operations require a current database under CAR Part 91
Out-of-date databases risk using outdated or incorrect procedures
Updates are delivered via over-the-air or USB/card downloads
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors influencing GNSS integrity, continuity and availability, including: pilot data input
concept: Pilot data input is probably the biggest source of real-world GNSS errors in NZ airspace. Typing the wrong waypoint identifier, selecting a similar-named waypoint in a different country, loading the wrong approach, or failing to chain waypoints in the right order are all common mistakes that send aeroplanes off course. The solution is rigorous cross-checking: enter the flight plan carefully, check each waypoint against the chart, verify the loaded route distance and bearing match the planned values, and re-check before every phase. 'Trust but verify' applies every time — the box does exactly what you tell it to, which is not necessarily what you meant.
exam_tip: Pilot data input error = biggest real-world GNSS hazard. Verify every waypoint. Check loaded track and distance against planned values.
key_facts: Pilot input errors are the most common cause of GNSS navigation problems
Wrong waypoint identifiers, similar names in other countries, wrong approach loaded
Failure to chain waypoints in the correct order
Mitigation: verify each waypoint, cross-check loaded route against chart
'Trust but verify' — the box does what you tell it
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors influencing GNSS integrity, continuity and availability, including: GNSS/aircraft system integration
concept: GNSS integration with other aircraft systems introduces its own risks and its own safeguards. Modern IFR installations integrate GNSS with the autopilot, flight director, EFIS displays, traffic alerting and terrain warning systems — all of which depend on accurate position data. If the GNSS is wrong or loses RAIM, every dependent system is affected. Good integration means clear, consistent flagging across all the dependent displays when GNSS is unavailable; poor integration can mean a single GNSS fault leaves you with subtly wrong guidance on multiple systems. IFR certification of GNSS installations (under TSO-C129, TSO-C145/146 and so on) specifies the integration requirements, including how failures must be annunciated and how the system must behave when integrity is lost.
exam_tip: GNSS is integrated with autopilot, FD, EFIS, TAWS, traffic systems. All depend on accurate GNSS. Loss of integrity affects everything downstream.
key_facts: GNSS is typically integrated with autopilot, flight director, EFIS, TAWS and traffic systems
All dependent systems use the GNSS position
A GNSS fault affects everything downstream
Good integration: clear, consistent annunciation of GNSS problems
IFR certification under TSO-C129, TSO-C145/146 specifies integration requirements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91; standard GNSS theory

syllabus_desc: Describe Performance Based Navigation (PBN)
concept: Performance Based Navigation (PBN) is a modern approach to defining navigation capability not by the equipment you have in the cockpit, but by the level of performance you can achieve. Instead of saying 'you must have a VOR and an ADF', PBN says 'you must be able to stay within X nautical miles of track for Y percent of the flight time, and here's what we'll call that level of performance'. The performance requirement is measured by position accuracy, integrity (confidence in the position), continuity (reliable delivery of the service), availability, and functionality. The idea is to let operators use whatever equipment meets the performance standard, and to standardise how those standards are defined globally so an approach designed in Europe works the same way in New Zealand.
exam_tip: PBN = performance-based. Specifies what you must achieve (accuracy, integrity, continuity, etc.), not what equipment you must have.
key_facts: Defines navigation by the performance achieved, not by specific equipment
Performance covers accuracy, integrity, continuity, availability and functionality
Allows multiple equipment options to satisfy the same specification
Global standard via ICAO Doc 9613
Replaces older equipment-specific navigation requirements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the following elements of PBN: the Navigation Specification
concept: A Navigation Specification is the documented set of performance and functional requirements for a particular navigation application. It tells you what performance the aircraft and crew must deliver — for example, RNP 1 means your total system error must be within 1 nm for 95% of the flight time, with certain functional capabilities like continuous position monitoring and alerting. Each specification has a name (RNAV 10, RNP 1, RNP APCH, etc.), a defined accuracy level, and a list of required aircraft capabilities and pilot procedures. The navigation specification is what you comply with to fly a specific PBN procedure.
exam_tip: Navigation Specification = the rule book. Defines accuracy, required functions, and crew procedures for a specific PBN operation.
key_facts: Documents the performance and functional requirements of a PBN operation
Includes required accuracy, integrity and continuity levels
Specifies aircraft equipment requirements and pilot procedures
Examples: RNAV 10, RNAV 5, RNP 1, RNP APCH, RNP AR APCH
Defined by ICAO in Doc 9613
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the following elements of PBN: the Navigation Aid Infrastructure
concept: The Navigation Aid Infrastructure is the physical collection of ground-based, space-based or airborne systems that provide the signals on which a PBN operation relies. For a GNSS-based PBN operation, the infrastructure includes the satellite constellation, any augmentation systems (SBAS, GBAS), and the ground monitoring network. For a DME/DME based PBN operation, it's the network of DME ground stations. A navigation specification typically lists what infrastructure is acceptable — some specifications are GNSS-only, others allow DME/DME, and a few allow multiple options.
exam_tip: Navigation Aid Infrastructure = the signal sources that support the navigation (satellites, DMEs, VORs). A given spec lists which are acceptable.
key_facts: Physical signal sources that enable the navigation
Includes GNSS constellation, SBAS, GBAS, DME stations, VORs
Different PBN specifications accept different infrastructure
Some specs are GNSS-only, others allow multiple sources
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the following elements of PBN: the Navigation Application
concept: The Navigation Application is the actual use of PBN within a particular airspace or phase of flight. This might be 'RNP 1 in the departure phase at Auckland', or 'RNAV 5 enroute across NZ airspace', or 'RNP APCH to runway 23 at Queenstown'. The application pairs a navigation specification with a specific operational environment — it's where the theory meets the real aeroplane in real airspace. Charts and procedures reference the navigation application being used so both the pilot and ATC know exactly what performance level is in play at any moment.
exam_tip: Navigation Application = where and when a specification is actually used (e.g. RNP APCH at Queenstown). The operational deployment of the spec.
key_facts: The operational use of a navigation specification in a specific airspace
Links a specification to a particular phase of flight or procedure
Examples: RNAV 5 enroute, RNP 1 SID, RNP APCH to a specific runway
Published on charts and in AIP NZ for each procedure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: 2D instrument approach operation
concept: A 2D instrument approach operation provides lateral (horizontal) guidance only — no vertical guidance from the navigation system. You track the lateral path using the navigation equipment, but you descend using step-down fixes on the chart and your altimeter, maintaining each minimum altitude until past the next fix. The classic example is an NDB or VOR non-precision approach, and in PBN terms, an LNAV-only RNP approach. Most IFR pilots still fly plenty of 2D approaches — they're perfectly safe when flown with discipline, but they require more workload than a 3D approach because you're doing the vertical profile manually.
exam_tip: 2D approach = lateral guidance only. Descend using step-down fixes and altimeter. LNAV, NDB, VOR approaches are 2D.
key_facts: Provides lateral guidance only
Vertical descent uses step-down fixes and the altimeter
Examples: NDB, VOR, LNAV-only RNP approaches
Also called a non-precision approach
Requires manual vertical profile management
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: 3D instrument approach operation
concept: A 3D instrument approach operation provides both lateral and vertical guidance from the navigation system. The classic example is an ILS, but in PBN terms it also includes LPV, LNAV/VNAV (baro-VNAV) and LP with vertical guidance. You fly down a defined glidepath to a decision altitude, and the system shows you both lateral and vertical deviation continuously. 3D approaches are lower-workload and generally have lower minima than equivalent 2D procedures because the continuous vertical guidance reduces the risk of descending prematurely.
exam_tip: 3D approach = lateral + vertical guidance. Examples: ILS, LPV, LNAV/VNAV (baro-VNAV). Lower workload, usually lower minima than 2D.
key_facts: Provides both lateral and vertical guidance continuously
Examples: ILS, LPV, LNAV/VNAV
Pilot follows a defined glidepath to a decision altitude
Lower workload than 2D approaches
Typically offers lower approach minima
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: ABAS
concept: ABAS stands for Aircraft-Based Augmentation System. It's the category of GNSS augmentation that happens inside the aircraft itself, without any external corrections. The two main ABAS functions are RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring) and barometric aiding. ABAS is what makes a standard IFR GPS receiver usable for navigation without needing a wide-area or local correction service. Most NZ IFR GNSS operations currently rely on ABAS.
exam_tip: ABAS = Aircraft-Based Augmentation. RAIM + barometric aiding. No external infrastructure needed.
key_facts: Aircraft-Based Augmentation System
Relies on receiver-internal integrity monitoring
Main functions: RAIM and barometric aiding
No external correction service required
Most current NZ IFR GNSS ops use ABAS
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: SBAS
concept: SBAS stands for Satellite-Based Augmentation System. It uses geostationary satellites to broadcast correction data and integrity information to aircraft receivers over a wide area — continental or regional in scale. Examples include WAAS (USA), EGNOS (Europe), MSAS (Japan), and GAGAN (India). SBAS brings GNSS accuracy down to around 1–3 metres and provides integrity flagging that's fast enough to support precision approaches (LPV). NZ does not currently have operational SBAS coverage, which limits the use of LPV approaches locally, though that's expected to change.
exam_tip: SBAS = Satellite-Based Augmentation. Geostationary satellites broadcast corrections. Examples: WAAS, EGNOS. Enables LPV approaches. NZ has no SBAS yet.
key_facts: Satellite-Based Augmentation System
Uses geostationary satellites to broadcast correction data
Improves accuracy to approximately 1–3 metres
Provides fast integrity alerting for precision approaches
Examples: WAAS (USA), EGNOS (Europe), MSAS (Japan)
NZ does not currently have operational SBAS coverage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: GBAS
concept: GBAS stands for Ground-Based Augmentation System. It uses ground reference receivers at a specific aerodrome to calculate precise local corrections, which are then broadcast by a VHF datalink to aircraft in the approach area. Because it's local, the corrections are highly accurate — GBAS can support Category I, II and III precision approaches (GBAS Landing System, or GLS), offering capability equivalent to ILS without the ground infrastructure of a full ILS. NZ doesn't currently have operational GBAS installations, but several aerodromes around the world use it.
exam_tip: GBAS = Ground-Based Augmentation. Local corrections from aerodrome-based reference stations. Supports GLS precision approaches up to Cat III.
key_facts: Ground-Based Augmentation System
Uses ground reference receivers at a specific aerodrome
Broadcasts corrections via VHF datalink
Supports Category I/II/III precision approaches (GLS)
Not currently operational in NZ
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: GBNA
concept: GBNA stands for Ground-Based Navigation Aids — the traditional network of VORs, DMEs, NDBs and ILSs that were the backbone of IFR navigation before GNSS. In a PBN context, GBNA infrastructure can still support certain navigation specifications (DME/DME based RNAV, for example), though most modern PBN is GNSS-based. GBNA networks are being progressively rationalised around the world as GNSS becomes the primary navigation source, but they remain important as a backup and for areas of poor GNSS coverage.
exam_tip: GBNA = Ground-Based Navigation Aids. VOR, DME, NDB, ILS. Still used for backup and DME/DME based PBN.
key_facts: Ground-Based Navigation Aids
Includes VOR, DME, NDB and ILS networks
Historically the primary IFR navigation infrastructure
Now supplemental to GNSS for most PBN operations
Still important as backup and for some DME/DME PBN
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: AMoN
concept: AMoN stands for Aerodrome Moving Navigation — though the term is not widely used and you're unlikely to meet it outside a handful of technical texts. In the AC 61-17 context it refers to moving map or navigation information presented for aerodrome operations, typically surface guidance for taxi and ground movement using GNSS data. Treat it as a specialised term for surface navigation displays and move on; it's a knowledge item, not a routine operational one.
exam_tip: AMoN: aerodrome moving navigation — a specialised term for GNSS-derived surface guidance. Uncommon in day-to-day ops.
key_facts: Aerodrome Moving Navigation
Specialised term for surface guidance using GNSS
Uncommon in routine operations
Listed in AC 61-17 as a definitional item
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: APCH
concept: APCH is just the ICAO/PBN shorthand for 'approach'. You'll see it in specification names — RNP APCH is the generic PBN specification for instrument approaches, and LNAV, LNAV/VNAV and LPV minima all fall under RNP APCH. When a chart or AIP entry refers to 'APCH' it means the approach phase of flight or the approach segment of a procedure.
exam_tip: APCH = Approach. Used in specification names like RNP APCH.
key_facts: Shorthand for 'approach'
Used in specification names (RNP APCH)
Refers to the approach phase of flight or procedure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: RNAV
concept: RNAV stands for Area Navigation. It's a navigation method that allows aircraft to fly any desired flight path within the coverage of ground- or space-based navaids, or within the limits of self-contained aids, or a combination of these. RNAV doesn't require you to fly directly from one navaid to another — you can fly between arbitrary waypoints. In PBN specifications, RNAV specs (RNAV 10, RNAV 5, RNAV 2, RNAV 1) have accuracy requirements (95% of flight time within the specified nm value of track) but do not require onboard performance monitoring and alerting.
exam_tip: RNAV = Area Navigation. Fly between any waypoints, not station-to-station. PBN RNAV specs don't require onboard performance monitoring.
key_facts: Area Navigation
Allows flight between arbitrary waypoints without overflying navaids
RNAV PBN specs: RNAV 10, RNAV 5, RNAV 2, RNAV 1
Accuracy requirement: 95% within the specified value (nm) of track
Does not require onboard performance monitoring and alerting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: RNP
concept: RNP stands for Required Navigation Performance. It's an RNAV operation with an additional key requirement: onboard monitoring and alerting of the navigation performance. That is, the aircraft system must be capable of checking its own accuracy and warning the crew if performance drops below the specified level. RNP specs (RNP 4, RNP 2, RNP 1, RNP APCH, RNP AR APCH) all carry this onboard integrity requirement, which makes them a step up in capability from plain RNAV. RNP also uses the numerical value differently — RNP 1 means the system must contain the aircraft within 1 nm of track for 95% of the flight time with onboard monitoring to flag violations.
exam_tip: RNP = RNAV + onboard performance monitoring and alerting. Key word: monitoring. RNP specs can flag their own failures.
key_facts: Required Navigation Performance
RNAV with onboard performance monitoring and alerting
Key differentiator from plain RNAV
Examples: RNP 4, RNP 2, RNP 1, RNP APCH, RNP AR APCH
System must alert the crew if the required performance is not being met
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: AR
concept: AR stands for Authorisation Required. An RNP AR procedure (typically RNP AR APCH) is a special performance-based approach that requires specific operator, aircraft and crew approval before it can be flown. These procedures use very tight performance tolerances (sometimes down to RNP 0.1) and can include complex curved segments (radius-to-fix legs) for approaches into constrained terrain — Queenstown in NZ is an example of an aerodrome where AR procedures make otherwise unfeasible approaches possible. AR approvals involve training, equipment certification, and specific operational procedures; you can't just fly them because you feel like it.
exam_tip: AR = Authorisation Required. Special approach procedures needing specific operator, aircraft and crew approval. Often very tight RNP (e.g. 0.1).
key_facts: Authorisation Required
Special PBN procedures requiring specific approval to fly
Typically RNP AR APCH
Can use very tight RNP values (down to 0.1)
May include curved (radius-to-fix) segments
Used for approaches into terrain-constrained aerodromes such as Queenstown
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: ANP
concept: ANP stands for Actual Navigation Performance — the performance the system is currently delivering, as measured by the aircraft's own navigation computer. For PBN operations you compare ANP to the required performance (RNP): as long as ANP is better (smaller) than RNP, you're meeting the requirement. If ANP exceeds RNP, the system flags a warning and you must respond — typically by reverting to another navigation method or breaking off the approach.
exam_tip: ANP = Actual Navigation Performance (what you're achieving). Must be better than RNP (what's required). ANP > RNP = alert.
key_facts: Actual Navigation Performance
What the aircraft is currently delivering
Compared against the required navigation performance (RNP)
ANP better than RNP = meeting the requirement
ANP worse than RNP = alert and pilot action required
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: EPU
concept: EPU stands for Estimated Position Uncertainty — the system's own estimate of how accurately it knows its position, typically expressed as a 95% confidence radius in nm (so an EPU of 0.3 means the system believes it's within 0.3 nm of the shown position 95% of the time). EPU is what the FMS uses as its ANP for PBN performance checking. Some systems call it a similar name — HFOM (horizontal figure of merit) or HPL (horizontal protection level) — but the idea is the same.
exam_tip: EPU = Estimated Position Uncertainty. System's own 95% confidence in its position. Used as ANP for PBN checks.
key_facts: Estimated Position Uncertainty
System's own estimate of position accuracy
Typically expressed as a 95% confidence radius in nm
Used as ANP for PBN performance monitoring
Similar concepts: HFOM, HPL
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: Total System Error (TSE)
concept: Total System Error (TSE) is the total error in the aircraft's position relative to the intended path. It has three main components: Path Definition Error (errors in defining the path in the database or by the pilot), Flight Technical Error (how well the pilot or autopilot is actually flying the commanded path), and Navigation System Error (the accuracy of the navigation system's own position solution). RNP specifications require that TSE stay within the specified value for 95% of the flight time. That's why pilot technique matters — a sloppy flight technical error can push TSE out even if the GNSS is perfect.
exam_tip: TSE = Path Definition + Flight Technical + Navigation System errors. 95% within the RNP value is the requirement. Pilot flying contributes directly.
key_facts: Total System Error: total position error from the intended path
Path Definition Error: errors in defining the path (database or pilot input)
Flight Technical Error: how well the aircraft is actually flown to the commanded path
Navigation System Error: the navigation system's own position accuracy
Must be within the RNP specification for 95% of flight time
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: LP
concept: LP stands for Localizer Performance. It's a 2D (lateral guidance only) approach minima line within an RNP APCH procedure that uses SBAS-derived lateral guidance equivalent in angular precision to a localiser. It doesn't include vertical guidance, so it's a non-precision approach with tighter lateral tolerances than LNAV. Used in areas with SBAS coverage, it gives you better lateral accuracy than LNAV but still requires you to fly the vertical profile yourself.
exam_tip: LP = Localizer Performance. Lateral-only RNP APCH minima using SBAS. Tighter lateral accuracy than LNAV, no vertical guidance.
key_facts: Localizer Performance
A 2D minima line within an RNP APCH procedure
Uses SBAS for lateral guidance
Lateral accuracy equivalent to a localiser
No vertical guidance — pilot flies the profile
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: LPV
concept: LPV stands for Localizer Performance with Vertical guidance. It's an SBAS-based approach with both lateral and vertical guidance, equivalent in performance to an ILS Category I. On a chart, LPV minima appear alongside LNAV and LNAV/VNAV minima on an RNP APCH plate — if your aircraft has SBAS capability and SBAS is available, you can fly to the LPV minima, which are typically the lowest. NZ does not currently have SBAS coverage, so LPV minima aren't usable here yet.
exam_tip: LPV = Localizer Performance with Vertical guidance. SBAS-based 3D approach, equivalent to ILS Cat I. Not yet available in NZ (no SBAS).
key_facts: Localizer Performance with Vertical guidance
SBAS-based 3D approach
Equivalent to ILS Category I
Lowest minima line on a typical RNP APCH plate
Not usable in NZ until SBAS coverage is operational
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: LNAV
concept: LNAV stands for Lateral Navigation. It's the basic 2D (lateral only) minima line on an RNP APCH procedure — you get lateral guidance from GNSS and fly the vertical profile using step-down fixes and the altimeter. LNAV is the 'fallback' minima on an RNP approach that's usable by any IFR-approved GNSS without SBAS or advanced VNAV capability. In NZ you'll most often fly RNP approaches to LNAV minima.
exam_tip: LNAV = Lateral Navigation only. 2D minima line on RNP APCH. The most widely usable PBN approach minima in NZ.
key_facts: Lateral Navigation only
2D minima line on an RNP APCH procedure
Uses GNSS for lateral guidance
Vertical profile is flown using step-down fixes
Usable by basic IFR-approved GNSS installations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: VNAV
concept: VNAV stands for Vertical Navigation. It's the aircraft system's ability to compute and follow a vertical path — a glidepath — between waypoints. In PBN, VNAV usually refers to the vertical component of an LNAV/VNAV approach, where the aircraft follows a computed glidepath (not an ILS-style radio glideslope, but a calculated path based on position and altitude).
exam_tip: VNAV = Vertical Navigation. The aircraft computes and flies a vertical path. Usually paired with LNAV in LNAV/VNAV approaches.
key_facts: Vertical Navigation
Aircraft system computes and follows a vertical path
Glidepath is calculated by the FMS, not received from a radio signal
Usually paired with LNAV for LNAV/VNAV approaches
Supports 3D approach operations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: Baro-VNAV
concept: Baro-VNAV is VNAV that uses the aircraft's barometric altimeter as the vertical reference. The FMS computes a geometric glidepath between waypoints, but the actual vertical position feedback comes from the baro altimeter corrected for local QNH. The big advantage is that it doesn't need SBAS — any aircraft with a proper FMS and altimeter can fly a baro-VNAV approach. The catch is that cold temperatures cause the altimeter to under-read (the indicated altitude is higher than the true altitude), which makes the aircraft fly lower than the intended glidepath on a cold day. Baro-VNAV approaches therefore have a published minimum temperature below which they cannot be flown without correction.
exam_tip: Baro-VNAV uses the altimeter as vertical reference. Cold temps = altimeter under-reads = aircraft flies low. Minimum temperature restriction.
key_facts: Barometric VNAV
Uses the aircraft altimeter as the vertical reference
Does not require SBAS
Cold temperatures cause the aircraft to fly below the intended glidepath
Published minimum temperature for each procedure
Common in NZ for 3D RNP approaches
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: Fly-by waypoints
concept: A fly-by waypoint is a waypoint that the aircraft does not physically overfly — instead, the FMS anticipates the turn and starts turning early, cutting the corner so the track joins the next leg smoothly without overshooting. Most enroute and SID/STAR waypoints are fly-by, because a smooth curve through them keeps the aeroplane on track without any wings-level overshoots. The size of the cut depends on bank angle, ground speed and the amount of turn required.
exam_tip: Fly-by waypoint: FMS turns early and cuts the corner to join the next leg smoothly. Never actually overflown.
key_facts: FMS anticipates the turn and starts turning before the waypoint
The aircraft cuts the corner and joins the next leg smoothly
Never overflies the waypoint itself
Most enroute, SID and STAR waypoints are fly-by
Turn lead-in depends on bank angle, ground speed and turn magnitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: Fly-over waypoints
concept: A fly-over waypoint is one the aircraft must physically pass directly over before beginning the turn to the next leg. The FMS delays the turn until the aircraft has actually crossed the waypoint, which means a wider, often less smooth turn than a fly-by. Fly-over waypoints are used where terrain, obstacles or procedural reasons require the aeroplane to actually overfly a point — for example, at an initial approach fix where you must cross the fix before turning onto the intermediate segment.
exam_tip: Fly-over waypoint: aircraft physically overflies it before turning. Used where terrain/obstacle/procedure requires crossing the point.
key_facts: Aircraft must physically pass directly over the waypoint
Turn begins only after crossing the waypoint
Typically produces a wider turn than a fly-by
Used where terrain or procedural reasons require overflight
Shown distinctly from fly-by waypoints on charts (usually with a circle around the symbol)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: Desired track (DTK)
concept: Desired Track (DTK) is the magnetic track you should be following between two waypoints to remain on the intended path. The FMS or GPS display usually shows DTK alongside your current track (TRK). When DTK equals TRK you're flying straight along the intended path; when they differ, you've drifted. DTK is essentially 'where you should be pointed over the ground', and it's a key reference for cross-checking that you're flying the route you think you're flying.
exam_tip: DTK = Desired Track. The magnetic track you should be flying to stay on the intended route. Compare with actual TRK.
key_facts: Desired Track — the magnetic track between two waypoints
Shown on the FMS/GPS display
Compared with the aircraft's actual track (TRK)
Key reference for verifying you're flying the intended route
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: Track to fix (TF)
concept: Track to Fix (TF) is a path terminator describing a leg that simply tracks a straight line between one waypoint and the next. It's the most common leg type in an RNP procedure — most enroute and arrival legs are TF. The FMS knows the start waypoint, the end waypoint, and the straight track between them, and its job is to keep you on that line until you reach the end waypoint.
exam_tip: TF = Track to Fix. Straight leg between two waypoints. Most common leg type in a PBN procedure.
key_facts: Path terminator describing a straight leg between two waypoints
Most common leg type in PBN procedures
FMS follows a straight line between the two fixes
Used throughout enroute and most arrival/departure legs
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: Direct to fix (DF)
concept: Direct to Fix (DF) is a path terminator where the aircraft flies directly from its present position to a specified waypoint. Unlike TF, there's no defined start point — the leg begins wherever you are when you activate it. DF legs are commonly used after a 'direct to' command from ATC, or as the initial leg of a missed approach ('on missed approach, direct to XYZ'). The FMS simply computes a new straight line from where you are now to the target waypoint.
exam_tip: DF = Direct to Fix. Goes direct from present position to a named waypoint. Used for 'direct to' clearances and missed approach initial legs.
key_facts: Path terminator: direct from present position to a named waypoint
No defined start point — begins wherever the aircraft currently is
Used after 'direct to' ATC clearances
Common initial leg of a missed approach procedure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: Course to fix (CF)
concept: Course to Fix (CF) is a path terminator where the aircraft flies a specified course (magnetic bearing) until arriving at a specified fix. Unlike TF, the leg is defined by a course rather than by a start waypoint — so the aircraft joins the course from wherever it is and then flies that course until reaching the fix. CF legs are often used in approach procedures where you need to intercept a specific course on the way to the fix — for example, the final approach course of an RNP approach.
exam_tip: CF = Course to Fix. Fly a specified course until arriving at a named fix. Used for intercepting specific final approach courses.
key_facts: Path terminator: fly a specific course until reaching a named fix
Defined by course and end fix, not by start waypoint
Aircraft joins the course from wherever it currently is
Common in final approach segments
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: Radius to fix (RF)
concept: Radius to Fix (RF) is a path terminator describing a constant-radius curved leg ending at a specified fix. Instead of flying a series of short straight legs to approximate a curve, the FMS flies a true circular arc at a specified radius, giving a smooth curved ground track. RF legs are the cornerstone of RNP AR APCH procedures, allowing constant-radius turns through constrained airspace — Queenstown is a good example of an NZ aerodrome where RF legs enable approaches through mountain terrain. They require a capable FMS and specific aircraft certification to fly.
exam_tip: RF = Radius to Fix. Constant-radius curved leg ending at a fix. Used in RNP AR approaches (e.g. Queenstown) to curve through terrain.
key_facts: Path terminator: constant-radius curved leg to a specified fix
Produces a true circular arc on the ground
Used in RNP AR APCH procedures
Enables approaches through terrain-constrained airspace
Requires capable FMS and specific aircraft certification
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between RNAV and RNP navigation specifications
concept: The fundamental difference between RNAV and RNP is onboard performance monitoring and alerting. An RNAV specification (RNAV 10, RNAV 5, RNAV 1, etc.) requires the aircraft to achieve a certain navigation accuracy for 95% of the flight time but does not require the aircraft to monitor that performance in real time.
