An RNP specification (RNP 4, RNP 2, RNP 1, RNP APCH, RNP AR APCH) adds the requirement that the aircraft system continuously monitors the actual navigation performance and alerts the crew if it falls below the required level. In practice that means RNP operations can be conducted in airspace where no external surveillance is available (e.g. oceanic) because the aircraft itself can flag a performance failure.
exam_tip: RNAV vs RNP: both have accuracy requirements, but only RNP requires onboard performance monitoring and alerting. That's the key difference.
key_facts: Both RNAV and RNP specify accuracy requirements (95% within a stated value)
Only RNP requires onboard performance monitoring and alerting
RNAV: RNAV 10, RNAV 5, RNAV 2, RNAV 1
RNP: RNP 4, RNP 2, RNP 1, RNP APCH, RNP AR APCH
RNP operations can be used where external surveillance is unavailable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain where the various navigation specifications are applied
concept: Each navigation specification is applied in airspace and phases of flight suited to its performance level. RNAV 10 (formerly RNP 10) is used in oceanic airspace where lateral separations are large. RNAV 5 is used for enroute flight in continental airspace. RNAV 2 and RNAV 1 are used in terminal and enroute operations requiring tighter accuracy. RNP 4 is oceanic/remote. RNP 2 is enroute continental or remote with monitoring. RNP 1 is used in terminal areas for SIDs and STARs. RNP APCH covers most instrument approaches. RNP AR APCH is reserved for special authorised approaches in difficult terrain. AIP NZ ENR 1.1 identifies which specifications are in use in which parts of NZ airspace.
exam_tip: Match the spec to the airspace: oceanic uses big numbers (RNAV 10, RNP 4), enroute uses middle (RNAV 5, RNP 2), terminal uses small (RNAV 1, RNP 1), approach uses RNP APCH or RNP AR APCH.
key_facts: Oceanic: RNAV 10, RNP 4
Continental enroute: RNAV 5, RNP 2
Terminal/SIDs/STARs: RNAV 1, RNP 1
Approach: RNP APCH
Special AR approaches: RNP AR APCH
NZ-specific application is published in AIP NZ ENR 1.1
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the construction of a PBN containment area
concept: A PBN containment area is the volume of airspace around the nominal track that the aircraft is expected to stay within with high confidence. For a given RNP value, the containment area is typically defined at 2 × RNP either side of the nominal track for 99.999% of the flight time — so an RNP 1 operation has an approximately ±2 nm containment area. Obstacle clearance is designed so that any object outside the containment area is clear of the aircraft; the containment area is what protects you from terrain and obstacles while using PBN. Procedure designers use the containment area to determine how close to terrain a PBN procedure can be routed.
exam_tip: PBN containment area = 2 × RNP either side of track, for 99.999% of flight time. That's the protected volume. Obstacle clearance is based on it.
key_facts: The airspace volume within which an aircraft is guaranteed to remain with high confidence
Typically defined as 2 × RNP either side of nominal track
Confidence level is 99.999% of flight time
Used by procedure designers to ensure obstacle clearance
Defines how closely PBN routes can pass terrain and obstacles
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; ICAO Doc 9613 PBN Manual; AIP NZ ENR; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: Automatic Dependent Surveillance - Broadcast (ADS-B)
concept: Automatic Dependent Surveillance – Broadcast (ADS-B) is a surveillance system where each aeroplane automatically and continuously broadcasts its own position (derived from GNSS), velocity, altitude and identification to anyone listening — ATC ground stations, other ADS-B equipped aircraft, and traffic display systems. 'Automatic' because it does it without any interrogation; 'Dependent' because it depends on the aircraft's own navigation system (usually GNSS); 'Broadcast' because the signal goes out to everyone, not in reply to a specific query. ADS-B is the backbone of modern surveillance in many parts of the world, including New Zealand, where it's been mandatory in controlled airspace since late 2022.
exam_tip: ADS-B: Automatic (no interrogation), Dependent (on GNSS), Broadcast (to everyone). Aircraft reports its own position. Mandatory in NZ controlled airspace.
key_facts: Automatic Dependent Surveillance – Broadcast
Aircraft continuously broadcasts its own position, velocity, altitude and ID
Position is derived from the aircraft's own GNSS
No ground interrogation required
Mandatory in NZ controlled airspace from late 2022
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; Airways NZ surveillance documentation

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by: Multilateration
concept: Multilateration (MLAT) is a surveillance technique that uses the time difference of arrival of a signal at multiple ground receivers to calculate the position of the source. An aeroplane's transponder reply (Mode A/C or Mode S) reaches different ground stations at slightly different times depending on the aircraft's location. By comparing those tiny time differences across multiple receivers (at least three for 2D, four for 3D), the system triangulates the aircraft's position without needing the aircraft to report its own GNSS position. Multilateration works with the existing transponder in the aeroplane, so no new airborne equipment is needed — just the ground infrastructure.
exam_tip: Multilateration: ground stations compute aircraft position from time-difference-of-arrival of its transponder replies. No GNSS input from the aircraft needed.
key_facts: Surveillance based on time difference of arrival at multiple ground receivers
Uses existing transponder replies (Mode A/C/S)
Needs at least 3 receivers for 2D, 4 for 3D
Does not depend on the aircraft's GNSS
Works with any transponder-equipped aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; Airways NZ surveillance documentation

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of: ADS-B
concept: ADS-B's function is to provide surveillance data to ATC and other aircraft in a way that's cheaper, faster-updating and more accurate than traditional radar. ATC uses ADS-B-OUT data to display aircraft positions on the controller's screen, often with update rates around once per second — much faster than radar's 4–12 second sweeps. ADS-B-IN equipped aircraft can use the same data to display nearby traffic in the cockpit (TIS-B style traffic displays). In New Zealand, ADS-B is the primary surveillance source across most controlled airspace, replacing and supplementing radar coverage.
exam_tip: ADS-B function: fast, accurate surveillance for ATC and cockpit traffic displays. ~1 Hz update vs 4–12 s for radar. Primary surveillance in NZ now.
key_facts: Provides surveillance data to ATC and other aircraft
Update rate around once per second (vs 4–12 s for radar)
More accurate than radar, as position comes from GNSS
Supports in-cockpit traffic displays (ADS-B IN / TIS-B)
Primary surveillance source in most NZ controlled airspace
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; Airways NZ surveillance documentation

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of: Multilateration
concept: Multilateration fills the surveillance gaps where ADS-B or radar aren't cost-effective or feasible. It provides accurate position reporting for any transponder-equipped aircraft without requiring ADS-B equipage — handy for legacy aircraft that haven't been upgraded. It's often used in approach and airport surface surveillance (wide-area multilateration for approach, multilateration networks for airport movement areas), and at some smaller aerodromes or in terrain-constrained areas where building a full radar installation isn't practical. In NZ, multilateration complements ADS-B and radar in some approach and terminal areas.
exam_tip: Multilateration function: fills gaps where ADS-B or radar don't reach. Works with existing transponders. Used at some approach and airport areas.
key_facts: Provides surveillance where ADS-B or radar is not feasible
Works with any transponder-equipped aircraft
Used for wide-area approach surveillance and airport surface movement
Complements ADS-B and radar in NZ
No new airborne equipment required
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; Airways NZ surveillance documentation

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic operating principle of: ADS-B
concept: ADS-B works like this: the airborne ADS-B OUT transmitter takes position data from the aircraft's GNSS receiver (plus altitude from the altimeter/encoder, velocity from the GNSS or inertial system, and identity from the transponder setup) and broadcasts a message on 1090 MHz (1090ES — the extended squitter from the Mode S transponder) or on a separate frequency for some UAT systems. The broadcast is unsolicited and roughly once per second. Ground stations pick up the broadcasts, decode them, and pass the position data to the ATC system. Other ADS-B-IN equipped aircraft pick up the same broadcasts directly and display the traffic on their own displays.
exam_tip: ADS-B OUT: GNSS position + altitude + velocity + ID, broadcast on 1090ES (most common) every ~1 second. Everyone listening picks it up.
key_facts: ADS-B OUT broadcasts on 1090 MHz (1090ES) for most aviation use
Transmits GNSS-derived position, altitude, velocity and identity
Broadcast rate approximately once per second
Received directly by ground stations and nearby ADS-B IN aircraft
Message content is unsolicited — no ATC interrogation required
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; Airways NZ surveillance documentation

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic operating principle of: Multilateration
concept: Multilateration uses a network of synchronised ground receivers listening for transponder replies. When an aeroplane's transponder transmits (either in response to an interrogator or via ADS-B OUT), the reply arrives at each receiver at a slightly different time because the distances from the aircraft to each receiver are different. The ground system precisely measures the time of arrival at each receiver and uses the time differences (differences, not absolute times — which is why precise synchronisation between receivers is critical) to solve for the aircraft's position. With three receivers and good geometry you get a 2D position; with four, a full 3D position.
exam_tip: Multilateration: synchronised ground receivers measure time differences of transponder reply arrival. Three receivers = 2D, four = 3D position.
key_facts: Network of precisely synchronised ground receivers
Measures time of arrival of each transponder reply at each receiver
Uses time differences (TDOA) to solve for position
Requires at least 3 receivers for 2D, 4 for 3D
Precise receiver synchronisation is critical
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; Airways NZ surveillance documentation

syllabus_desc: Explain the inputs and outputs of: ADS-B
concept: ADS-B OUT inputs: GNSS position, pressure altitude (from the encoder), velocity (from GNSS or other sources), aircraft identification (set up when the transponder/ADS-B unit is installed), and various status flags (emergency, airborne/on-ground). ADS-B OUT output: a continuous 1090 MHz extended squitter broadcast containing all the above. ADS-B IN inputs: the 1090 MHz signals received from other aircraft or from ground TIS-B/FIS-B services. ADS-B IN outputs: traffic displays on a cockpit screen, weather and advisory information (on systems that support FIS-B).
exam_tip: ADS-B OUT in: GNSS + altitude + velocity + ID. Out: 1090ES broadcast. ADS-B IN in: 1090ES signals. Out: traffic and advisory displays in cockpit.
key_facts: ADS-B OUT inputs: GNSS position, pressure altitude, velocity, ID, status flags
ADS-B OUT output: 1090 MHz extended squitter broadcast
ADS-B IN inputs: 1090 MHz signals from other aircraft and ground services
ADS-B IN outputs: traffic display, weather/advisory information (FIS-B where supported)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; Airways NZ surveillance documentation

syllabus_desc: Explain the inputs and outputs of: Multilateration
concept: Multilateration inputs: the transponder replies from each aeroplane in the coverage area (Mode A/C or Mode S replies, or ADS-B extended squitters), received at multiple ground receivers with precise time stamps. The system also needs the precisely known positions of each receiver and a synchronised time reference. Multilateration outputs: the calculated position (and derived velocity) of each aircraft, fed into the ATC surveillance system and displayed to the controller just like a radar or ADS-B target.
exam_tip: Multilateration inputs: transponder replies at multiple receivers, plus receiver positions and time sync. Output: computed aircraft position for ATC display.
key_facts: Inputs: transponder replies (Mode A/C/S) received at multiple ground stations
Requires precise receiver position and time synchronisation
Outputs: computed aircraft position and velocity
Fed into ATC surveillance displays alongside radar and ADS-B data
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; Airways NZ surveillance documentation

syllabus_desc: Explain the limitations of: ADS-B
concept: ADS-B's main limitation is its dependence on GNSS. If the aircraft's GNSS fails or loses RAIM, ADS-B OUT can't broadcast valid position — and at that point, ATC loses you (unless they have backup surveillance like MLAT or radar). ADS-B is also vulnerable to spoofing and jamming because the signals are unencrypted and the protocol was not designed with security as a priority. Line-of-sight limits ground station coverage in mountainous terrain. Finally, ADS-B only sees aircraft with working ADS-B OUT — an unequipped or malfunctioning aircraft is invisible to the ADS-B network, which is why compliance mandates matter.
exam_tip: ADS-B limitations: depends on GNSS (no GNSS = no position), unencrypted (spoofing), line-of-sight, only sees equipped aircraft.
key_facts: Depends entirely on aircraft GNSS integrity
Unencrypted — vulnerable to spoofing and jamming
Line-of-sight propagation limits ground coverage
Only detects aircraft with functioning ADS-B OUT
GNSS outage = loss of ADS-B position
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; Airways NZ surveillance documentation

syllabus_desc: Explain the limitations of: Multilateration
concept: Multilateration's main limitations are infrastructure and geometry. You need a network of synchronised receivers with good geometric spread; in areas with only two or three receivers or poor geometry, position accuracy degrades or you can't get a 3D fix at all. Coverage is limited to where the receiver network exists — this is why multilateration is typically used for specific terminal or airport areas rather than wide-area enroute surveillance. Like any transponder-based system, it depends on the aircraft having a working transponder; if the transponder is off, broken or not configured correctly, multilateration can't see it. Finally, very dense traffic can overload the receivers just as with SSR saturation.
exam_tip: Multilateration limitations: needs a dense synchronised receiver network, limited coverage, depends on working transponder, degrades with poor geometry.
key_facts: Requires a network of synchronised receivers with good geometry
Coverage limited to where the receiver network exists
Accuracy degrades with fewer receivers or poor geometric spread
Depends on the aircraft having a working transponder
Can saturate with very dense traffic
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17; AIP NZ ENR; Airways NZ surveillance documentation
